item.dc:title: Total Synthesis of rac‐Keramamine C Enabled by Intrinsic H‐Bond‐Induced Conformational Fixation of Amides

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Stereoselective Synthesis of Glycomimetic Amines From Biomass‐Derived Cyrene
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 18, 14 May 2026.
item.dc:description:
2,3,4‐Trideoxy‐2‐mannosamines, including the unsubstituted one, are accessible as the only endo diastereoisomers by reductive amination of the green solvent Cyrene, derived from pyrolysis of cellulose containing waste biomass. The products are useful chiral synthetic intermediate to nitrogenated glycomimetics.
Cyrene, a low‐cost commercial chiral compound widely available from the pyrolysis of cellulose containing materials including waste biomass, was employed in the preparation of secondary amines. The process exhibited nearly complete stereoselectivity for the d‐manno configured amines (endo amines) within the 2,3,4‐trideoxy hexose structure. Debenzylation of the N‐benzylamine via catalytic hydrogenation provided the first synthesis of the diastereomerically pure primary amine, employable in turn for accessing the same (2S)‐configured endo amines exclusively through reductive amination with aldehydes. This unprecedented access to pure endo amine furnishes a versatile chiral synthetic intermediate to nitrogenated glycomimetics.
item.content:encoded:
2,3,4-Trideoxy-2-mannosamines, including the unsubstituted one, are accessible as the only endo diastereoisomers by reductive amination of the green solvent Cyrene, derived from pyrolysis of cellulose containing waste biomass. The products are useful chiral synthetic intermediate to nitrogenated glycomimetics.


Cyrene, a low-cost commercial chiral compound widely available from the pyrolysis of cellulose containing materials including waste biomass, was employed in the preparation of secondary amines. The process exhibited nearly complete stereoselectivity for the d-manno configured amines (endo amines) within the 2,3,4-trideoxy hexose structure. Debenzylation of the N-benzylamine via catalytic hydrogenation provided the first synthesis of the diastereomerically pure primary amine, employable in turn for accessing the same (2S)-configured endo amines exclusively through reductive amination with aldehydes. This unprecedented access to pure endo amine furnishes a versatile chiral synthetic intermediate to nitrogenated glycomimetics.
item.dc:creator: Debora Pratesi, Marta Rigacci, Francesca Cardona, Camilla Matassini, Andrea Goti
item.dc:title: Stereoselective Synthesis of Glycomimetic Amines From Biomass‐Derived Cyrene

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Transmembrane Transport Activity and Selectivity of Amide‐Based Macrocyclic Anion Receptors of Different Sizes
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 18, 14 May 2026.
item.dc:description:
Amide‐based macrocycles of varying sizes and acyclic bis‐amides were synthesised and tested for Cl– transmembrane transport activity and selectivity versus OH–, NO3–, and HCO3–. Pre‐organised and smaller fluorinated macrocycles showed the highest rates, while simple bis‐amides mimicking half of the macrocycles had only slightly reduced activity. Furthermore, anion transport selectivity could be tuned by cavity size and aryl electron density.
Synthetic anion receptors can transport anions across lipid bilayer membranes. Many anion transporters have been reported, including macrocyclic compounds. However, there are no systematic studies on how the size of macrocycles impacts their anion transport activity and selectivity. Therefore, we prepared eight amide‐based macrocyclic compounds with ring sizes ranging from 18 to 26 atoms, as well as four bis‐amides. We studied these compounds as anion receptors using chloride titrations and molecular modelling, and as anion transporters using liposomes with various fluorescent probes. Neither the size of the macrocycles nor the affinity for chloride was found to be a determining factor for chloride transport activity; pre‐organisation appears to play a more important role. Fluorination was found to have a clear positive effect on anion transport rates, with the bis‐amides performing surprisingly well compared to the macrocycles. The smallest non‐fluorinated macrocycle exhibited selectivity for chloride over hydroxide and nitrate, whereas the pentafluorophenyl bis‐amide demonstrated effective transport of bicarbonate and nitrate, likely due to anion–π interactions in addition to hydrogen bonding. The insights from this study will shape the design of future anionophores.
item.content:encoded:
Amide-based macrocycles of varying sizes and acyclic bis-amides were synthesised and tested for Cl– transmembrane transport activity and selectivity versus OH–, NO3 –, and HCO3 –. Pre-organised and smaller fluorinated macrocycles showed the highest rates, while simple bis-amides mimicking half of the macrocycles had only slightly reduced activity. Furthermore, anion transport selectivity could be tuned by cavity size and aryl electron density.


Synthetic anion receptors can transport anions across lipid bilayer membranes. Many anion transporters have been reported, including macrocyclic compounds. However, there are no systematic studies on how the size of macrocycles impacts their anion transport activity and selectivity. Therefore, we prepared eight amide-based macrocyclic compounds with ring sizes ranging from 18 to 26 atoms, as well as four bis-amides. We studied these compounds as anion receptors using chloride titrations and molecular modelling, and as anion transporters using liposomes with various fluorescent probes. Neither the size of the macrocycles nor the affinity for chloride was found to be a determining factor for chloride transport activity; pre-organisation appears to play a more important role. Fluorination was found to have a clear positive effect on anion transport rates, with the bis-amides performing surprisingly well compared to the macrocycles. The smallest non-fluorinated macrocycle exhibited selectivity for chloride over hydroxide and nitrate, whereas the pentafluorophenyl bis-amide demonstrated effective transport of bicarbonate and nitrate, likely due to anion–π interactions in addition to hydrogen bonding. The insights from this study will shape the design of future anionophores.
item.dc:creator: Karolis Norvaisa, Hasna Sherin Ummat, Lau Halgreen, Giulia Piras, Alice Giaprakis, Alessio Cataldo, Stephen J. Butler, Giacomo Picci, Claudia Caltagirone, Hennie Valkenier
item.dc:title: Transmembrane Transport Activity and Selectivity of Amide‐Based Macrocyclic Anion Receptors of Different Sizes

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Palladium‐Catalyzed β‐C(sp3)‐H Arylation of Alanine at the Internal Position of Peptides
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 18, 14 May 2026.
item.dc:description:
We herein report a backbone protection strategy, which enabled Pd‐catalyzed β‐C(sp3)‐H arylation of internal alanine in peptides. Control experiment demonstrated that the backbone protecting group (p‐methoxybenzyl, PMB) not only controlled the position‐selectivity, but also improved the reactivity of C–H arylation. Further removal of the protecting group under mild and oxidative conditions to afford the backbone‐unprotected peptides efficiently exhibited the synthetic utility of the developed protocol.
Late‐stage peptide modification by C–H functionalization has been extensively investigated in the past decades. However, transition metal‐catalyzed C(sp3)‐H functionalization of amino acid residues at the internal positions of peptides remains underdeveloped due to the inhibition effect of peptide bonds (secondary amide). In the context, we herein report a backbone protection strategy, which enabled Pd‐catalyzed β‐C(sp3)‐H arylation of internal alanine in peptides. Control experiment demonstrated that the backbone protecting group (p‐methoxybenzyl, PMB) not only controlled the position‐selectivity, but also improved the reactivity of C–H arylation. Further removal of the protecting group under mild and oxidative conditions to afford the backbone‐unprotected peptides efficiently exhibited the synthetic utility of the developed protocol.
item.content:encoded:
We herein report a backbone protection strategy, which enabled Pd-catalyzed β-C(sp3)-H arylation of internal alanine in peptides. Control experiment demonstrated that the backbone protecting group (p-methoxybenzyl, PMB) not only controlled the position-selectivity, but also improved the reactivity of C–H arylation. Further removal of the protecting group under mild and oxidative conditions to afford the backbone-unprotected peptides efficiently exhibited the synthetic utility of the developed protocol.


Late-stage peptide modification by C–H functionalization has been extensively investigated in the past decades. However, transition metal-catalyzed C(sp3)-H functionalization of amino acid residues at the internal positions of peptides remains underdeveloped due to the inhibition effect of peptide bonds (secondary amide). In the context, we herein report a backbone protection strategy, which enabled Pd-catalyzed β-C(sp3)-H arylation of internal alanine in peptides. Control experiment demonstrated that the backbone protecting group (p-methoxybenzyl, PMB) not only controlled the position-selectivity, but also improved the reactivity of C–H arylation. Further removal of the protecting group under mild and oxidative conditions to afford the backbone-unprotected peptides efficiently exhibited the synthetic utility of the developed protocol.
item.dc:creator: Meng‐Jie Liao, Yun‐Fan Lu, Bo Yao
item.dc:title: Palladium‐Catalyzed β‐C(sp3)‐H Arylation of Alanine at the Internal Position of Peptides

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Base‐Promoted Thioester Synthesis from Acyl Fluorides Using Sulfonyl Hydrazides as Thiol Surrogates
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 18, 14 May 2026.
item.dc:description:
A base‐promoted thioester synthesis from acyl fluorides is achieved using sulfonyl hydrazides as odorless thiol surrogates. The reaction proceeds in i‐PrOAc with K2CO3 to deliver a broad range of aryl thioesters in up to 88% yield across 28 examples, and mechanistic studies support an ionic acyl‐substitution step followed by a radical N2‐extrusion pathway for CS bond formation.
A method for synthesizing thioesters from acyl fluorides has been developed using sulfonyl hydrazides as thiol surrogates. The reaction proceeds under base‐promoted conditions, generating a diverse array of aryl thioesters in good‐to‐excellent yields with high functional‐group tolerance. Mechanistic studies reveal an initial ionic acyl substitution to form acyl sulfonyl hydrazide intermediates, followed by a radical process involving N2 extrusion and the recombination of acyl and thio fragments to form the CS bond.
item.content:encoded:
A base-promoted thioester synthesis from acyl fluorides is achieved using sulfonyl hydrazides as odorless thiol surrogates. The reaction proceeds in i-PrOAc with K2CO3 to deliver a broad range of aryl thioesters in up to 88% yield across 28 examples, and mechanistic studies support an ionic acyl-substitution step followed by a radical N2-extrusion pathway for CS bond formation.


A method for synthesizing thioesters from acyl fluorides has been developed using sulfonyl hydrazides as thiol surrogates. The reaction proceeds under base-promoted conditions, generating a diverse array of aryl thioesters in good-to-excellent yields with high functional-group tolerance. Mechanistic studies reveal an initial ionic acyl substitution to form acyl sulfonyl hydrazide intermediates, followed by a radical process involving N2 extrusion and the recombination of acyl and thio fragments to form the CS bond.
item.dc:creator: Yejin Koo, Indrajit Karmakar, Chin‐Fa Lee, Sunwoo Lee
item.dc:title: Base‐Promoted Thioester Synthesis from Acyl Fluorides Using Sulfonyl Hydrazides as Thiol Surrogates

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Electrochemically Driven Domino Nucleophilic Addition/Diels–Alder Reaction: From 2‐Aminophenol Derivatives to Bridged Tricyclic Systems
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 18, 14 May 2026.
item.dc:description:
This electrochemical methodology allows the preparation of bridged tricyclic scaffolds through a rapid increase in the molecular complexity of simple and readily accessible starting materials. Alcohols proved to be the most efficient nucleophiles, as well as 6‐chloropurine. Plausibly, the process involves an anodic oxidative dearomatization to generate the key o‐quinone‐type intermediate.
The electrochemical methodology here reported allows the preparation of bridged tricyclic scaffolds through a rapid increase in the molecular complexity of simple and readily accessible starting materials. Standard conditions employing graphite as anode, nickel foam as cathode, and triflic acid as additive provided the target aza‐caged structures in good yields. Alcohols proved to be the most efficient nucleophiles, affording the desired products in modest yields, while nitrogen‐based nucleophiles generally resulted in decomposition, with the notable exception of 6‐chloropurine, which gave the desired product in moderate yield. Plausibly, the process involves an anodic oxidative dearomatization that generates an o‐quinone‐type intermediate, which undergoes nucleophilic addition followed by an intramolecular Diels–Alder reaction. Cyclic voltammetry confirmed the aminophenol as the electroactive species, supporting the proposed mechanistic pathway.
item.content:encoded:
This electrochemical methodology allows the preparation of bridged tricyclic scaffolds through a rapid increase in the molecular complexity of simple and readily accessible starting materials. Alcohols proved to be the most efficient nucleophiles, as well as 6-chloropurine. Plausibly, the process involves an anodic oxidative dearomatization to generate the key o-quinone-type intermediate.


The electrochemical methodology here reported allows the preparation of bridged tricyclic scaffolds through a rapid increase in the molecular complexity of simple and readily accessible starting materials. Standard conditions employing graphite as anode, nickel foam as cathode, and triflic acid as additive provided the target aza-caged structures in good yields. Alcohols proved to be the most efficient nucleophiles, affording the desired products in modest yields, while nitrogen-based nucleophiles generally resulted in decomposition, with the notable exception of 6-chloropurine, which gave the desired product in moderate yield. Plausibly, the process involves an anodic oxidative dearomatization that generates an o-quinone-type intermediate, which undergoes nucleophilic addition followed by an intramolecular Diels–Alder reaction. Cyclic voltammetry confirmed the aminophenol as the electroactive species, supporting the proposed mechanistic pathway.
item.dc:creator: Emanuele Cartamina, Sara Colombo, Davide Spanu, Gianluigi Broggini, Camilla Loro
item.dc:title: Electrochemically Driven Domino Nucleophilic Addition/Diels–Alder Reaction: From 2‐Aminophenol Derivatives to Bridged Tricyclic Systems

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: MsOH‐Mediated Cascade Cyclization of O‐Homopropargyl Hydroxylamines with Aldehydes to Access 4‐Arylidene‐1,2‐Oxazines
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 18, 14 May 2026.
item.dc:description:
A Brønsted acid‐mediated approach for the synthesis of 4‐arylidene oxazines from O‐homopropargyl hydroxylamines was developed. Mechanistic studies indicate that the transformation involves alkyne–oximium cyclization, ring‐opening, and subsequent condensation. The synthetic utility of this protocol was demonstrated via the preparation of 1,4‐aminoalcohols and γ‐hydroxy ketones through the reduction of the 4‐arylidene oxazines.
Brønsted acid‐mediated synthesis of 4‐arylidene oxazines from O‐homopropargyl hydroxylamines is developed. A series of control experiments have been carried out, revealing that the formation of 4‐arylidene oxazines proceeds through an alkyne–oximium cyclization, ring‐opening reaction, and subsequent condensation. Synthetic versatility of the developed methodology was highlighted in the synthesis of 1,4‐aminoalcohol as well as γ‐hydroxy ketone by the reduction of 4‐arylidene oxazine.
item.content:encoded:
A Brønsted acid-mediated approach for the synthesis of 4-arylidene oxazines from O-homopropargyl hydroxylamines was developed. Mechanistic studies indicate that the transformation involves alkyne–oximium cyclization, ring-opening, and subsequent condensation. The synthetic utility of this protocol was demonstrated via the preparation of 1,4-aminoalcohols and γ-hydroxy ketones through the reduction of the 4-arylidene oxazines.


Brønsted acid-mediated synthesis of 4-arylidene oxazines from O-homopropargyl hydroxylamines is developed. A series of control experiments have been carried out, revealing that the formation of 4-arylidene oxazines proceeds through an alkyne–oximium cyclization, ring-opening reaction, and subsequent condensation. Synthetic versatility of the developed methodology was highlighted in the synthesis of 1,4-aminoalcohol as well as γ-hydroxy ketone by the reduction of 4-arylidene oxazine.
item.dc:creator: Santosh J. Gharpure, Kaushik C. Pansuriya, Juhi Pal
item.dc:title: MsOH‐Mediated Cascade Cyclization of O‐Homopropargyl Hydroxylamines with Aldehydes to Access 4‐Arylidene‐1,2‐Oxazines

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Preparation of α‐Mono‐Fluoromethyl Silanes via Nucleophilic Displacement on Chlorosilanes With LiCH2F
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 18, 14 May 2026.
item.dc:description:
The SiCl bond acts as a versatile platform for undergoing a formal nucleophilic substitution promoted by LiCH2F en route to extremely rare α‐monofluoromethyl‐silanes.
Though surprising – when compared with well‐established poly‐fluoromethyl silanes (e.g., Ruppert‐Prakash reagent, TMSCF3) – α‐mono‐fluoromethyl silanes are underrepresented motifs in chemical synthesis, having been – so far – reported no more than 2–3 examples. We propose herein the nucleophilic substitution on the silicon atom of chlorosilanes – with fluoromethyllithium (LiCH2F) – as a general strategy for their direct preparation. The procedure ‐ exhibiting remarkable dependance on steric elements across silicon – can be advantageously extended to prepare distinct IV‐row heteroatom‐centered analogues such as tin and germanium organometallics.
item.content:encoded:
The SiCl bond acts as a versatile platform for undergoing a formal nucleophilic substitution promoted by LiCH2F en route to extremely rare α-monofluoromethyl-silanes.


Though surprising – when compared with well-established poly-fluoromethyl silanes (e.g., Ruppert-Prakash reagent, TMSCF3) – α-mono-fluoromethyl silanes are underrepresented motifs in chemical synthesis, having been – so far – reported no more than 2–3 examples. We propose herein the nucleophilic substitution on the silicon atom of chlorosilanes – with fluoromethyllithium (LiCH2F) – as a general strategy for their direct preparation. The procedure - exhibiting remarkable dependance on steric elements across silicon – can be advantageously extended to prepare distinct IV-row heteroatom-centered analogues such as tin and germanium organometallics.
item.dc:creator: Alberto Nardi, Davide Castiglione, Margherita Miele, Laura Castoldi, Vittorio Pace
item.dc:title: Preparation of α‐Mono‐Fluoromethyl Silanes via Nucleophilic Displacement on Chlorosilanes With LiCH2F

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Near‐Infrared‐Absorbing α‐Functionalized TCBD‐BODIPYs: Synthesis, Photophysical, and Electrochemical Properties
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 18, 14 May 2026.
item.dc:description:
A set of α‐functionalized meso‐anisyl boron‐dipyrromethene (BODIPYs) was synthesized via Sonogashira cross‐coupling and [2 + 2] cycloaddition‐retroelectrocyclization reaction. The photophysical, electrochemical, and computational studies of the ethynyl‐bridged α‐BODIPYs (BDS1 and BDS3) and mono‐1,1,4,4‐tetracyanobutadiene (TCBD)‐incorporated α‐BODIPYs (BDS2 and BDS4) were investigated. The TCBD‐incorporated BODIPYs exhibit a broad absorption band spanning 620–1000 nm.
Boron‐dipyrromethene (BODIPY) dyes absorb in the visible region; however, expanding their optical absorption into the near‐infrared (NIR) window is beneficial for optoelectronic applications. Herein, we report a set of α‐functionalized meso‐anisyl BODIPYs, BDS1–BDS4, synthesized via Sonogashira cross‐coupling and [2 + 2] cycloaddition‐retroelectrocyclization reaction. The photophysical, electrochemical, and computational studies of the ethynyl‐bridged α‐BODIPYs (BDS1 and BDS3) and mono‐1,1,4,4‐tetracyanobutadiene (TCBD)‐incorporated α‐BODIPYs (BDS2 and BDS4) are investigated. The TCBD‐incorporated BODIPYs exhibit a broad absorption band between 620 and 1000 nm, with an absorption maximum above 700 nm. The TCBD‐BODIPYs (BDS2 and BDS4) display about 40 nm red shift in absorption maxima compared to the ethynyl‐bridged BODIPYs (BDS1 and BDS3). The electrochemical studies reveal that the TCBD unit in BDS2 and BDS4 significantly lowers the LUMO energy levels, leading to smaller energy gaps for BDS2 and BDS4 compared to their corresponding ethynyl‐bridged BODIPYs, BDS1 and BDS3. The computational studies were performed to optimize the geometry and estimate the electrostatic potential of the α‐BODIPYs. This study highlights the effect of an additional acceptor TCBD on the photophysical and electrochemical properties of α‐functionalized BODIPYs, providing valuable insights for designing new NIR‐absorbing molecules for optoelectronic applications.
item.content:encoded:
A set of α-functionalized meso-anisyl boron-dipyrromethene (BODIPYs) was synthesized via Sonogashira cross-coupling and [2 + 2] cycloaddition-retroelectrocyclization reaction. The photophysical, electrochemical, and computational studies of the ethynyl-bridged α-BODIPYs (BDS1 and BDS3) and mono-1,1,4,4-tetracyanobutadiene (TCBD)-incorporated α-BODIPYs (BDS2 and BDS4) were investigated. The TCBD-incorporated BODIPYs exhibit a broad absorption band spanning 620–1000 nm.


Boron-dipyrromethene (BODIPY) dyes absorb in the visible region; however, expanding their optical absorption into the near-infrared (NIR) window is beneficial for optoelectronic applications. Herein, we report a set of α-functionalized meso-anisyl BODIPYs, BDS1–BDS4, synthesized via Sonogashira cross-coupling and [2 + 2] cycloaddition-retroelectrocyclization reaction. The photophysical, electrochemical, and computational studies of the ethynyl-bridged α-BODIPYs (BDS1 and BDS3) and mono-1,1,4,4-tetracyanobutadiene (TCBD)-incorporated α-BODIPYs (BDS2 and BDS4) are investigated. The TCBD-incorporated BODIPYs exhibit a broad absorption band between 620 and 1000 nm, with an absorption maximum above 700 nm. The TCBD-BODIPYs (BDS2 and BDS4) display about 40 nm red shift in absorption maxima compared to the ethynyl-bridged BODIPYs (BDS1 and BDS3). The electrochemical studies reveal that the TCBD unit in BDS2 and BDS4 significantly lowers the LUMO energy levels, leading to smaller energy gaps for BDS2 and BDS4 compared to their corresponding ethynyl-bridged BODIPYs, BDS1 and BDS3. The computational studies were performed to optimize the geometry and estimate the electrostatic potential of the α-BODIPYs. This study highlights the effect of an additional acceptor TCBD on the photophysical and electrochemical properties of α-functionalized BODIPYs, providing valuable insights for designing new NIR-absorbing molecules for optoelectronic applications.
item.dc:creator: Amiy Krishna, Abhishek Kumar, Rajneesh Misra
item.dc:title: Near‐Infrared‐Absorbing α‐Functionalized TCBD‐BODIPYs: Synthesis, Photophysical, and Electrochemical Properties

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: A Practical and Flexible De Novo Synthesis of Deoxygenated Carbasugars and Their Cyclitol Epoxides
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 18, 14 May 2026.
item.dc:description:
A de novo synthesis route comprising enantioselective Brown crotylation and ring‐closing metathesis as the key steps enables the synthesis of a‐ and b‐carba‐paratose, a‐ and b‐carba‐colitose as well as four configurational deoxycyclophellitol isosteres.
Literature on carbasugar and cyclitol epoxide glycomimetics is largely biased towards fully hydroxylated cyclitol structures. Partially deoxygenated sugars of varying configuration are widespread in nature and merit translation into the corresponding carbasugars and cyclitol epoxides as well. For this purpose, we developed a de novo synthesis route comprising enantioselective Brown crotylation and ring‐closing metathesis as the key steps and enabling the synthesis of α‐ and β‐carba‐paratose, α‐ and β‐carba‐colitose as well as four configurational deoxycyclophellitol isosteres in a strategy that allows the synthesis of other configurational isomers.
item.content:encoded:
A de novo synthesis route comprising enantioselective Brown crotylation and ring-closing metathesis as the key steps enables the synthesis of a- and b-carba-paratose, a- and b-carba-colitose as well as four configurational deoxycyclophellitol isosteres.


Literature on carbasugar and cyclitol epoxide glycomimetics is largely biased towards fully hydroxylated cyclitol structures. Partially deoxygenated sugars of varying configuration are widespread in nature and merit translation into the corresponding carbasugars and cyclitol epoxides as well. For this purpose, we developed a de novo synthesis route comprising enantioselective Brown crotylation and ring-closing metathesis as the key steps and enabling the synthesis of α- and β-carba-paratose, α- and β-carba-colitose as well as four configurational deoxycyclophellitol isosteres in a strategy that allows the synthesis of other configurational isomers.
item.dc:creator: Yevhenii Radchenko, Nick D. F. Puijmbroeck, Sanne M. Verduin, Jeroen D. C. Codée, Herman S. Overkleeft
item.dc:title: A Practical and Flexible De Novo Synthesis of Deoxygenated Carbasugars and Their Cyclitol Epoxides

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: A Direct Method to Prepare Zwitterionic Organotrifluoroborates: Ammonium and Sulfonium Derivatives
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 18, 14 May 2026.
item.dc:description:
Organotrifluoroborates find applications in synthesis, diagnostics, and theranostics. This study outlines a one‐step reaction for producing ammonium and sulfonium trifluoroborate zwitterion derivatives. This method achieves satisfactory yields without the need for expensive reagents. The sulfonium derivative shows great stability, and it has been successfully applied to click‐chemistry reactions, demonstrating its practical usefulness.
Organotrifluoroborates are an important functional group that is used in synthesis, diagnostics, and, more recently, in theranostics. Here, we present a practical, one‐step synthesis of ammonium and sulfonium trifluoroborate zwitterionic derivatives by N‐ or S‐ alkylation of bromomethyltrifluoroborate. The reaction proceeds smoothly to produce the desired compounds with good to excellent yields. In particular, the preparation of some sulfonium trifluoroborate derivatives, which avoids the use of expensive or troublesome reagents, their complete spectroscopic characterization, and an example of their application in a click‐chemistry reaction are reported.
item.content:encoded:
Organotrifluoroborates find applications in synthesis, diagnostics, and theranostics. This study outlines a one-step reaction for producing ammonium and sulfonium trifluoroborate zwitterion derivatives. This method achieves satisfactory yields without the need for expensive reagents. The sulfonium derivative shows great stability, and it has been successfully applied to click-chemistry reactions, demonstrating its practical usefulness.


Organotrifluoroborates are an important functional group that is used in synthesis, diagnostics, and, more recently, in theranostics. Here, we present a practical, one-step synthesis of ammonium and sulfonium trifluoroborate zwitterionic derivatives by N- or S- alkylation of bromomethyltrifluoroborate. The reaction proceeds smoothly to produce the desired compounds with good to excellent yields. In particular, the preparation of some sulfonium trifluoroborate derivatives, which avoids the use of expensive or troublesome reagents, their complete spectroscopic characterization, and an example of their application in a click-chemistry reaction are reported.

item.dc:title: A Direct Method to Prepare Zwitterionic Organotrifluoroborates: Ammonium and Sulfonium Derivatives

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Dynamic Kinetic Resolution of Axially Chiral Heterobiaryl N‐Oxides via Peptide‐Catalyzed Aldol Reaction
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 18, 14 May 2026.
item.dc:description:
Resin‐supported tripeptide achieved the dynamic kinetic resolution in the catalytic aldol reaction of formylated heterobiaryl N‐oxides with high efficiency and enantioselectivity. The system features a broad substrate scope and a recyclable catalyst.
A dynamic kinetic resolution of axially chiral heterobiaryl N‐oxides has been developed via an asymmetric aldol reaction catalyzed by a resin‐supported d‐Pro‐Tyr‐Phe tripeptide. This approach provides a pathway to enantioenriched heterobiaryl N‐oxide derivatives possessing both axial and central chirality, affording products with up to 96% ee and 9:1 dr. The methodology demonstrates a broad and robust substrate scope, tolerating a variety of electron‐donating and electron‐withdrawing substituents. In addition, the immobilized catalyst exhibits recyclability for at least three cycles. This system was also applicable to the kinetic resolution of racemic axially chiral substrates.
item.content:encoded:
Resin-supported tripeptide achieved the dynamic kinetic resolution in the catalytic aldol reaction of formylated heterobiaryl N-oxides with high efficiency and enantioselectivity. The system features a broad substrate scope and a recyclable catalyst.


A dynamic kinetic resolution of axially chiral heterobiaryl N-oxides has been developed via an asymmetric aldol reaction catalyzed by a resin-supported d-Pro-Tyr-Phe tripeptide. This approach provides a pathway to enantioenriched heterobiaryl N-oxide derivatives possessing both axial and central chirality, affording products with up to 96% ee and 9:1 dr. The methodology demonstrates a broad and robust substrate scope, tolerating a variety of electron-donating and electron-withdrawing substituents. In addition, the immobilized catalyst exhibits recyclability for at least three cycles. This system was also applicable to the kinetic resolution of racemic axially chiral substrates.
item.dc:creator: Jiaqi Tian, Isao Yoshikawa, Akihiro Sakama, Kazuaki Kudo
item.dc:title: Dynamic Kinetic Resolution of Axially Chiral Heterobiaryl N‐Oxides via Peptide‐Catalyzed Aldol Reaction

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Selectfluor‐Promoted Transition Metal‐Free Tunable Reactivities of Thio‐Oxindoles: Synthesis of Disulfanes and 2‐Oxindoles
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 18, 14 May 2026.
item.dc:description:
A simple, metal‐free selectfluor‐mediated tunable protocol for synthesizing disulfanes and 2‐oxindoles is presented. The reaction proceeds under mild conditions with broad substrate scope, delivering products in good to excellent yields.
An efficient and operationally simple protocol for the synthesis of disulfanes and 2‐oxindoles mediated by selectfluor under metal‐free conditions is described here. The methodology proceeds under mild reaction conditions and exhibits broad substrate scope, delivering the corresponding disulfane and 2‐oxindole derivatives in good to excellent yields.
item.content:encoded:
A simple, metal-free selectfluor-mediated tunable protocol for synthesizing disulfanes and 2-oxindoles is presented. The reaction proceeds under mild conditions with broad substrate scope, delivering products in good to excellent yields.


An efficient and operationally simple protocol for the synthesis of disulfanes and 2-oxindoles mediated by selectfluor under metal-free conditions is described here. The methodology proceeds under mild reaction conditions and exhibits broad substrate scope, delivering the corresponding disulfane and 2-oxindole derivatives in good to excellent yields.

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Sulfoximidation of Aryl Alkenes via Oxidative CC Bond Cleavage
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 18, 14 May 2026.
item.dc:description:
A room‐temperature KI/TBHP‐promoted, oxidative CC bond cleavage followed by CN bond formation, cross‐coupling between aryl alkene and NH‐sulfoximines affording N‐aroylated sulfoximines is presented.
KI−mediated cross‐coupling between aryl alkenes and NH−sulfoximines under base‐ and metal‐free reaction conditions has been developed. This process involves the sequential oxidative cleavage of the CC double bond, followed by CN bond formation to synthesize biologically relevant N‐acyl sulfoximines. This protocol features a broad substrate scope, excellent functional group compatibility, and high yields. Mechanistic investigations indicate the intermediacy of benzaldehyde‐derived as an acyl radical and highlight the crucial role of atmospheric oxygen as the oxygen source.
item.content:encoded:
A room-temperature KI/TBHP-promoted, oxidative CC bond cleavage followed by CN bond formation, cross-coupling between aryl alkene and NH-sulfoximines affording N-aroylated sulfoximines is presented.


KI−mediated cross-coupling between aryl alkenes and NH−sulfoximines under base- and metal-free reaction conditions has been developed. This process involves the sequential oxidative cleavage of the CC double bond, followed by CN bond formation to synthesize biologically relevant N-acyl sulfoximines. This protocol features a broad substrate scope, excellent functional group compatibility, and high yields. Mechanistic investigations indicate the intermediacy of benzaldehyde-derived as an acyl radical and highlight the crucial role of atmospheric oxygen as the oxygen source.

item.dc:title: Sulfoximidation of Aryl Alkenes via Oxidative CC Bond Cleavage

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: 1,2,6‐Thiadiazin‐4‐ones: Robust Photocatalysts for the Green Aerobic Oxidation of Sulfides to Sulfoxides
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 18, 14 May 2026.
item.dc:description:
A new photocatalyst for the aerobic photochemical oxidation of sulfides to sulfoxides is described under really low catalyst loading (0.01 mol%) and methanol as the green solvent.
The chemoselective oxidation of sulfides to sulfoxides is accomplished using a new family of photocatalysts, namely 1,2,6‐thiadiazin‐4‐ones. Utilizing a low catalyst loading (0.01 mol%), a 440 nm lamp as the irradiation source, and molecular oxygen as the oxidant, this rapid method is applicable to a broad range of alkyl‐aryl, dialkyl, and diaryl sulfides. Additionally, the method demonstrates practical relevance for the industrially significant synthesis of pharmaceutical compounds, including modafinil and sulforaphane.
item.content:encoded:
A new photocatalyst for the aerobic photochemical oxidation of sulfides to sulfoxides is described under really low catalyst loading (0.01 mol%) and methanol as the green solvent.


The chemoselective oxidation of sulfides to sulfoxides is accomplished using a new family of photocatalysts, namely 1,2,6-thiadiazin-4-ones. Utilizing a low catalyst loading (0.01 mol%), a 440 nm lamp as the irradiation source, and molecular oxygen as the oxidant, this rapid method is applicable to a broad range of alkyl-aryl, dialkyl, and diaryl sulfides. Additionally, the method demonstrates practical relevance for the industrially significant synthesis of pharmaceutical compounds, including modafinil and sulforaphane.

item.dc:description: The Front Cover illustrates the formation of thioesters from benzoyl fluoride (2 equiv., left trees) and sulfonylhydrazide (right tree) under K2CO3 conditions. The merging branches symbolize molecular fusion, capturing the essence of the efficient thioester synthesis method for pharmaceutical applications. More information can be found in the Research Article by C.‐F. Lee, S. Lee and co‐workers (DOI: 10.1002/ejoc.202600009).

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Informatics‐Driven and Automated Optimization in Flow Electrochemical Synthesis
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 18, 14 May 2026.
item.dc:description:
This review highlights electrochemical flow synthesis integrated with informatics‐driven optimization platforms. Several methodologies, such as design of experiments, Gaussian process regression, Bayesian optimization, and high‐throughput experimentation, have been employed to accelerate reaction development in electrochemical synthesis.
Electrochemical synthesis has emerged as a powerful platform for environmentally sustainable chemical transformations. When integrated with flow chemistry, electrosynthetic processes exhibit enhanced scalability, making them suitable for industrial applications. Recently, the integration of electrochemical flow systems with informatics techniques has accelerated the optimization of reaction conditions. Data‐driven strategies facilitate rapid exploration of multidimensional parameter spaces, enabling identification of optimal reaction conditions with high efficiency. These advances have enabled the development of automated optimization systems. This review highlights recent progress in combining electrosynthesis, flow chemistry, and computational tools, focusing on representative examples that illustrate efficient optimization protocols and autonomous reaction development. By showcasing these developments, we discuss how the integration of these technologies is driving innovation in electrochemical synthesis.
item.content:encoded:
This review highlights electrochemical flow synthesis integrated with informatics-driven optimization platforms. Several methodologies, such as design of experiments, Gaussian process regression, Bayesian optimization, and high-throughput experimentation, have been employed to accelerate reaction development in electrochemical synthesis.


Electrochemical synthesis has emerged as a powerful platform for environmentally sustainable chemical transformations. When integrated with flow chemistry, electrosynthetic processes exhibit enhanced scalability, making them suitable for industrial applications. Recently, the integration of electrochemical flow systems with informatics techniques has accelerated the optimization of reaction conditions. Data-driven strategies facilitate rapid exploration of multidimensional parameter spaces, enabling identification of optimal reaction conditions with high efficiency. These advances have enabled the development of automated optimization systems. This review highlights recent progress in combining electrosynthesis, flow chemistry, and computational tools, focusing on representative examples that illustrate efficient optimization protocols and autonomous reaction development. By showcasing these developments, we discuss how the integration of these technologies is driving innovation in electrochemical synthesis.

item.dc:title: Informatics‐Driven and Automated Optimization in Flow Electrochemical Synthesis

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Chan–Lam Cross‐Coupling With Alkylboron Reagents: From Transmetallation to Radical Pathways
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 18, 14 May 2026.
item.dc:description:
Alkylative Chan–Lam coupling overcomes intrinsic transmetallation limitations of alkylboron reagents by engaging radical pathways. Recent advances reveal how radical and radical–polar crossover mechanisms enable mild C(sp3)—heteroatom bond formation. This minireview highlights key mechanistic insights and emerging strategies that transform alkylboron reagents into versatile coupling partners.
The Chan–Lam coupling offers a mild and versatile method for the arylation of heteronucleophiles and has found applications in diverse field of organic chemistry. However, the development of an alkylative variant of this transformation remains a major challenge. The main barrier is the transmetallation of boronic acid derivatives to a copper complex, which is facile with arylboronic acid derivatives but problematic for their alkyl counterpart. While some boronic acid derivatives, such as cyclopropyl derivatives, can undergo coupling under standard conditions, others require either forcing conditions or/and formation of a radical intermediate. Such radical‐based approaches have blossomed over recent years and now provide alternative and attractive strategies for heteroatom alkylation. Moreover, since alkyl residues—and particularly alkyl amines—are often unstable under strong oxidative conditions, adopting a milder radical process is appealing. This review focuses on the development of an alkylative variant of the Chan–Lam coupling, from early examples with cyclopropylboronic acid, to radical transfer‐based strategies. Mechanistic aspects are discussed, highlighting how the discovery that alkylative Chan–Lam coupling can proceed via a radical intermediate has transformed the approach, sparking further research in the field.
item.content:encoded:
Alkylative Chan–Lam coupling overcomes intrinsic transmetallation limitations of alkylboron reagents by engaging radical pathways. Recent advances reveal how radical and radical–polar crossover mechanisms enable mild C(sp3)—heteroatom bond formation. This minireview highlights key mechanistic insights and emerging strategies that transform alkylboron reagents into versatile coupling partners.


The Chan–Lam coupling offers a mild and versatile method for the arylation of heteronucleophiles and has found applications in diverse field of organic chemistry. However, the development of an alkylative variant of this transformation remains a major challenge. The main barrier is the transmetallation of boronic acid derivatives to a copper complex, which is facile with arylboronic acid derivatives but problematic for their alkyl counterpart. While some boronic acid derivatives, such as cyclopropyl derivatives, can undergo coupling under standard conditions, others require either forcing conditions or/and formation of a radical intermediate. Such radical-based approaches have blossomed over recent years and now provide alternative and attractive strategies for heteroatom alkylation. Moreover, since alkyl residues—and particularly alkyl amines—are often unstable under strong oxidative conditions, adopting a milder radical process is appealing. This review focuses on the development of an alkylative variant of the Chan–Lam coupling, from early examples with cyclopropylboronic acid, to radical transfer-based strategies. Mechanistic aspects are discussed, highlighting how the discovery that alkylative Chan–Lam coupling can proceed via a radical intermediate has transformed the approach, sparking further research in the field.
item.dc:creator: Nicolas G.‐Simonian, Andrea Serafino, Luc Neuville, Géraldine Masson
item.dc:title: Chan–Lam Cross‐Coupling With Alkylboron Reagents: From Transmetallation to Radical Pathways

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Isotellurourea‐Catalyzed Enantioselective (4+2)‐Heterocycloadditions of But‐2‐ynoates and Michael Acceptors
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Isotelluroureas allow for the use of alkynoates for asymmetric (4+2)‐heterocycloadditions with different Michael acceptors.
Alk‐2‐ynoates are readily available starting materials which, in principle, can be utilized for cycloadditions in analogy to structurally similar allenoates. However, their significantly lower reactivity makes activation by established isothiourea and isoselenourea Lewis base catalysts difficult. Capitalizing on the higher nucleophilicity and basicity of our recently introduced class of isotellurourea catalysts we now succeeded in successfully employing alk‐2‐ynoates for asymmetric (4 + 2)‐heterocycloadditions as well, resulting in even improved enantioselectivities as compared to the use of analogous allenoates. These results demonstrate the high potential of the highly reactive isotelluroureas for the catalytic activation of less reactive starting materials where established catalyst classes come to their limits.
item.content:encoded:
Isotelluroureas allow for the use of alkynoates for asymmetric (4+2)-heterocycloadditions with different Michael acceptors.


Alk-2-ynoates are readily available starting materials which, in principle, can be utilized for cycloadditions in analogy to structurally similar allenoates. However, their significantly lower reactivity makes activation by established isothiourea and isoselenourea Lewis base catalysts difficult. Capitalizing on the higher nucleophilicity and basicity of our recently introduced class of isotellurourea catalysts we now succeeded in successfully employing alk-2-ynoates for asymmetric (4 + 2)-heterocycloadditions as well, resulting in even improved enantioselectivities as compared to the use of analogous allenoates. These results demonstrate the high potential of the highly reactive isotelluroureas for the catalytic activation of less reactive starting materials where established catalyst classes come to their limits.
item.dc:creator: Anna Scheucher, Mario Waser
item.dc:title: Isotellurourea‐Catalyzed Enantioselective (4+2)‐Heterocycloadditions of But‐2‐ynoates and Michael Acceptors

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Achieving Diverse Functionalizations of Electron‐Deficient Aryl Halides via Carbonate‐Mediated Electron‐Donor–Acceptor Complexes
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
We report a simple and practical electron donor–acceptor complex‐mediated method that employs inexpensive carbonate bases to activate electron‐deficient aryl iodides and bromides. The protocol enables diverse transformations, including arylation, dehalogenation, deuteration, chalcogenation, and borylation, providing a versatile platform for the functionalization of aryl halide substrates.
Aryl halides are excellent precursors for functional group interconversion due to their malleability and diversity. They are also among the most common building blocks due to their use in cross‐coupling reactions. Traditional methodologies for activating these motifs make use of expensive precious metals, such as palladium, and often require the use of complex and even more expensive ligands. Electron‐donor–acceptor complexes have recently gained attention as a new photo‐induced approach for the activation of various molecular motifs, including aryl halides. Herein, further extension of these methodologies led to the development of a straightforward and simple protocol that uses inexpensive carbonate bases for the activation of electron‐deficient aryl iodides and bromides. The procedure was employed for the arylation, dehalogenation, deuteration, chalcogenation, and borylation of aryl halide starting materials.
item.content:encoded:
We report a simple and practical electron donor–acceptor complex-mediated method that employs inexpensive carbonate bases to activate electron-deficient aryl iodides and bromides. The protocol enables diverse transformations, including arylation, dehalogenation, deuteration, chalcogenation, and borylation, providing a versatile platform for the functionalization of aryl halide substrates.


Aryl halides are excellent precursors for functional group interconversion due to their malleability and diversity. They are also among the most common building blocks due to their use in cross-coupling reactions. Traditional methodologies for activating these motifs make use of expensive precious metals, such as palladium, and often require the use of complex and even more expensive ligands. Electron-donor–acceptor complexes have recently gained attention as a new photo-induced approach for the activation of various molecular motifs, including aryl halides. Herein, further extension of these methodologies led to the development of a straightforward and simple protocol that uses inexpensive carbonate bases for the activation of electron-deficient aryl iodides and bromides. The procedure was employed for the arylation, dehalogenation, deuteration, chalcogenation, and borylation of aryl halide starting materials.
item.dc:creator: Krishnakumar Sachidanandan, Cole Stenftenagel, Athul Joshy, Sébastien Laulhé
item.dc:title: Achieving Diverse Functionalizations of Electron‐Deficient Aryl Halides via Carbonate‐Mediated Electron‐Donor–Acceptor Complexes

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Fast Reduction of Nitroalkenes Into Nitroalkanes Under Continuous‐Flow Chemical Conditions
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Flow chemical synthesis was employed for the reduction of nitroalkenes to the corresponding nitroalkanes using sodium borohydride as the reducing agent. The reaction proceeds in only 150 s, affording products from good‐to‐excellent yields (61–96%).
Herein, we describe an efficient and rapid continuous‐flow reduction of nitroalkenes to nitroalkanes. The reaction, promoted by sodium borohydride at room temperature, delivered the corresponding nitroalkanes in good‐to‐excellent yields, tolerating a broad range of functionalities, with a remarkably short residence time of only 150 s.
item.content:encoded:
Flow chemical synthesis was employed for the reduction of nitroalkenes to the corresponding nitroalkanes using sodium borohydride as the reducing agent. The reaction proceeds in only 150 s, affording products from good-to-excellent yields (61–96%).


Herein, we describe an efficient and rapid continuous-flow reduction of nitroalkenes to nitroalkanes. The reaction, promoted by sodium borohydride at room temperature, delivered the corresponding nitroalkanes in good-to-excellent yields, tolerating a broad range of functionalities, with a remarkably short residence time of only 150 s.
item.dc:creator: Iqra Munir, Gianmarco Simonelli, Marino Petrini, Alessandro Palmieri
item.dc:title: Fast Reduction of Nitroalkenes Into Nitroalkanes Under Continuous‐Flow Chemical Conditions

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Palladium‐Catalyzed Denitrogenative Sonogashira Coupling of Benzothiatriazine Dioxides
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A palladium‐catalyzed denitrogenative Sonogashira coupling of N‐alkyl benzothiatriazine dioxides has been developed for highly efficient access to o‐alkynylbenzenesulfonamides under mild conditions. The protocol features a wide substrate scope, good functional group compatibility, and tunable reactivity by copper cocatalyst and tetrabutyl ammonium bromide (TBAB) additive.
A denitrogenative Sonogashira coupling of N‐alkyl benzothiatriazine dioxides catalyzed by 1 mol% PdCl2(PPh3)2 or 1 mol% PdCl2(PPh3)2/CuI for aryl or alkyl alkynes, respectively, has been developed for highly efficient synthesis of o‐alkynylbenzenesulfonamides. The scope of this protocol has been demonstrated by >30 examples, with yields of up to 98%, showing a good compatibility with common functional groups. In addition, a one‐pot denitrogenative alkynylation/5‐exo‐dig cyclization of N‐alkyl benzothiatriazine dioxides with aryl alkynes is also described preliminarily under the palladium/copper bimetallic catalysis in the presence of tetrabutyl ammonium bromide (TBAB) additive.
item.content:encoded:
A palladium-catalyzed denitrogenative Sonogashira coupling of N-alkyl benzothiatriazine dioxides has been developed for highly efficient access to o-alkynylbenzenesulfonamides under mild conditions. The protocol features a wide substrate scope, good functional group compatibility, and tunable reactivity by copper cocatalyst and tetrabutyl ammonium bromide (TBAB) additive.


A denitrogenative Sonogashira coupling of N-alkyl benzothiatriazine dioxides catalyzed by 1 mol% PdCl2(PPh3)2 or 1 mol% PdCl2(PPh3)2/CuI for aryl or alkyl alkynes, respectively, has been developed for highly efficient synthesis of o-alkynylbenzenesulfonamides. The scope of this protocol has been demonstrated by >30 examples, with yields of up to 98%, showing a good compatibility with common functional groups. In addition, a one-pot denitrogenative alkynylation/5-exo-dig cyclization of N-alkyl benzothiatriazine dioxides with aryl alkynes is also described preliminarily under the palladium/copper bimetallic catalysis in the presence of tetrabutyl ammonium bromide (TBAB) additive.
item.dc:creator: Yuxian Wang, Shuyue Xiao, Gang Zou
item.dc:title: Palladium‐Catalyzed Denitrogenative Sonogashira Coupling of Benzothiatriazine Dioxides

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Electrophilic Iodination of Heterocyclic Compounds. Recent Advances 2008–2025: Part II
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Electrophilic iodination using molecular iodine and iodide sources represents an efficient approach for accessing valuable iodinated organic compounds. This review highlights recent advances in the iodination of heterocyclic compounds from 2008 to 2025, emphasizing the role of heteroaryl iodides as important synthetic intermediates for biologically active molecules, cross‐coupling chemistry, radical reactions, pharmaceutical development, and medical imaging.
The iodination of heterocyclic compounds has significant interest in synthetic organic chemistry, as it provides versatile and efficient pathways for the synthesis of a wide range of biologically active molecules. This review aims to highlight recent advances in the iodination of heterocyclic compounds using elemental iodine or iodide‐based reagents, covering developments reported over the past 17 years.
item.content:encoded:
Electrophilic iodination using molecular iodine and iodide sources represents an efficient approach for accessing valuable iodinated organic compounds. This review highlights recent advances in the iodination of heterocyclic compounds from 2008 to 2025, emphasizing the role of heteroaryl iodides as important synthetic intermediates for biologically active molecules, cross-coupling chemistry, radical reactions, pharmaceutical development, and medical imaging.


The iodination of heterocyclic compounds has significant interest in synthetic organic chemistry, as it provides versatile and efficient pathways for the synthesis of a wide range of biologically active molecules. This review aims to highlight recent advances in the iodination of heterocyclic compounds using elemental iodine or iodide-based reagents, covering developments reported over the past 17 years.

item.dc:title: Electrophilic Iodination of Heterocyclic Compounds. Recent Advances 2008–2025: Part II

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Gold(I)‐Mediated Reactivity of Allenes With Mesoionic Nucleophiles: A Computational Exploration
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
The regioselective gold(I)‐mediated reactivity of allenes with mesoionic nucleophiles has been evaluated through computational analysis of the mechanisms involved. Instead of typical (3 + 2) pathways, sydnones and münchnones favour the formal (3 + 3) cyclizations affording substituted dihydropyridines.
We explore the factors leading to regioselective nucleophilic additions in gold(I)‐mediated reactions with allenes through DFT analysis of model systems. The feasibility of intermolecular nucleophilic attack by sydnones and münchnones was evaluated and validated by comparison to several known nucleophiles, the transition states with münchnones being kinetically accessible. The resulting intermediates selectively undergo formal (3 + 3) cyclisations instead of the more usual (3 + 2) pathways, with substituted dihydropyridines as the ultimate products. We propose that the use of mesoionic reagents in gold(I)‐mediated processes is feasible, should regioselectively access different heterocycles, and could enable the development of new methodology in gold(I)‐mediated synthesis.
item.content:encoded:
The regioselective gold(I)-mediated reactivity of allenes with mesoionic nucleophiles has been evaluated through computational analysis of the mechanisms involved. Instead of typical (3 + 2) pathways, sydnones and münchnones favour the formal (3 + 3) cyclizations affording substituted dihydropyridines.


We explore the factors leading to regioselective nucleophilic additions in gold(I)-mediated reactions with allenes through DFT analysis of model systems. The feasibility of intermolecular nucleophilic attack by sydnones and münchnones was evaluated and validated by comparison to several known nucleophiles, the transition states with münchnones being kinetically accessible. The resulting intermediates selectively undergo formal (3 + 3) cyclisations instead of the more usual (3 + 2) pathways, with substituted dihydropyridines as the ultimate products. We propose that the use of mesoionic reagents in gold(I)-mediated processes is feasible, should regioselectively access different heterocycles, and could enable the development of new methodology in gold(I)-mediated synthesis.
item.dc:creator: Eduardo Garcia‐Padilla, Feliu Maseras
item.dc:title: Gold(I)‐Mediated Reactivity of Allenes With Mesoionic Nucleophiles: A Computational Exploration

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Enantioselective Access to Tetrahydroxanthenones Catalyzed by an Ionic Proline‐Histidine Dipeptide Derivative
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Dipeptide 1a catalyzed an enantioselective synthesis of tetrahydroxanthenones by the reaction of salicylaldehydes with cyclohex‐2‐en‐1‐one, in the presence of water and a Bronsted acid additive. Interestingly, the (L,L)‐dipeptide—derived using the natural forms of both amino acids—proved superior to its diastereomeric counterpart, suggesting an intrinsic role of the histidine chiral center also in the transformation, which presumably proceeds via an oxa‐Michael addition–aldol condensation sequence.
An enantioselective synthesis of tetrahydroxanthenones was achieved by the reaction of salicylaldehydes with cyclohex‐2‐en‐1‐one, catalyzed by a proline‐histidine dipeptide derived ion‐tagged organocatalyst in the presence of water and a Bronsted acid additive. The transformation, which involves an oxa‐Michael addition–aldol condensation sequence, occurs at room temperature and was successful over a range of salicylaldehydes, affording the heterotricyclic products in 33–99% yield and 29–95% ee.
item.content:encoded:
Dipeptide 1a catalyzed an enantioselective synthesis of tetrahydroxanthenones by the reaction of salicylaldehydes with cyclohex-2-en-1-one, in the presence of water and a Bronsted acid additive. Interestingly, the (L,L)-dipeptide—derived using the natural forms of both amino acids—proved superior to its diastereomeric counterpart, suggesting an intrinsic role of the histidine chiral center also in the transformation, which presumably proceeds via an oxa-Michael addition–aldol condensation sequence.


An enantioselective synthesis of tetrahydroxanthenones was achieved by the reaction of salicylaldehydes with cyclohex-2-en-1-one, catalyzed by a proline-histidine dipeptide derived ion-tagged organocatalyst in the presence of water and a Bronsted acid additive. The transformation, which involves an oxa-Michael addition–aldol condensation sequence, occurs at room temperature and was successful over a range of salicylaldehydes, affording the heterotricyclic products in 33–99% yield and 29–95% ee.
item.dc:creator: Akram Gulam Hussain Khan, Heena Inani, Ajit Kumar Jha, Srinivasan Easwar
item.dc:title: Enantioselective Access to Tetrahydroxanthenones Catalyzed by an Ionic Proline‐Histidine Dipeptide Derivative

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Gallium‐Catalyzed Tandem Carbonyl–Olefin Metathesis/Dehydrogenative Coupling Enables Rapid Access to Cyclopentenes
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A cationic gallium(III) catalyst enables rapid construction of substituted cyclopentenes via tandem carbonyl‐ene metathesis/Friedel–Crafts coupling. Electronic tuning of alcohol electrophiles allows access to diverse, densely functionalized rings in a single operation.
An efficient and versatile tandem carbonyl‐ene metathesis/Friedel–Crafts‐type coupling, catalyzed by the cationic gallium(III) complex [IPr·GaCl2][SbF6], provides rapid and modular access to cyclopentenes. This one‐pot protocol converts alkene‐ketones and activated alcohols into carbocycles bearing a tetrasubstituted C ═ C bond, in yields ranging from 21 to 68%, depending on the electronic nature of the electrophile generated from primary or secondary alcohols. Variation of substitution on the carbocyclic core enables access to a diverse array of functionalized ring systems from readily available precursors through a concise sequence.
item.content:encoded:
A cationic gallium(III) catalyst enables rapid construction of substituted cyclopentenes via tandem carbonyl-ene metathesis/Friedel–Crafts coupling. Electronic tuning of alcohol electrophiles allows access to diverse, densely functionalized rings in a single operation.


An efficient and versatile tandem carbonyl-ene metathesis/Friedel–Crafts-type coupling, catalyzed by the cationic gallium(III) complex [IPr·GaCl2][SbF6], provides rapid and modular access to cyclopentenes. This one-pot protocol converts alkene-ketones and activated alcohols into carbocycles bearing a tetrasubstituted C ═ C bond, in yields ranging from 21 to 68%, depending on the electronic nature of the electrophile generated from primary or secondary alcohols. Variation of substitution on the carbocyclic core enables access to a diverse array of functionalized ring systems from readily available precursors through a concise sequence.
item.dc:creator: Arnaud Espied, Djamila Azrou, Alexandre Djurovic, Régis Guillot, Aurélien Alix, Vincent Gandon, Christophe Bour
item.dc:title: Gallium‐Catalyzed Tandem Carbonyl–Olefin Metathesis/Dehydrogenative Coupling Enables Rapid Access to Cyclopentenes

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Shedding Light on Iron Catalysis
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Iron‐based photoredox catalysis enables precise, green synthesis under mild conditions with high selectivity and broad functional group tolerance. Due to iron's abundance, low cost, and tunable redox properties, it is promising for pharmaceuticals and materials. This review systematically covers 2019–2025 photoinduced iron systems, offering mechanistic insights and practical guidance for sustainable catalysis.
Iron‐based photoredox catalysis has become a powerful platform for achieving precise synthetic control while aligning with the goals of green chemistry. These reactions typically proceed under mild conditions and offer broad functional group compatibility along with high selectivity. Owing to iron's natural abundance, low cost, and tunable redox properties, iron photocatalysis is particularly attractive for applications in pharmaceutical synthesis and materials science. This review provides a systematic overview of photoinduced iron‐catalyzed systems reported from 2019 to 2025. By analyzing and comparing the mechanisms and key characteristics of different reaction pathways, it aims to offer both conceptual insights and practical guidance for the development of efficient, sustainable iron‐catalyzed transformations.
item.content:encoded:
Iron-based photoredox catalysis enables precise, green synthesis under mild conditions with high selectivity and broad functional group tolerance. Due to iron's abundance, low cost, and tunable redox properties, it is promising for pharmaceuticals and materials. This review systematically covers 2019–2025 photoinduced iron systems, offering mechanistic insights and practical guidance for sustainable catalysis.


Iron-based photoredox catalysis has become a powerful platform for achieving precise synthetic control while aligning with the goals of green chemistry. These reactions typically proceed under mild conditions and offer broad functional group compatibility along with high selectivity. Owing to iron's natural abundance, low cost, and tunable redox properties, iron photocatalysis is particularly attractive for applications in pharmaceutical synthesis and materials science. This review provides a systematic overview of photoinduced iron-catalyzed systems reported from 2019 to 2025. By analyzing and comparing the mechanisms and key characteristics of different reaction pathways, it aims to offer both conceptual insights and practical guidance for the development of efficient, sustainable iron-catalyzed transformations.

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Photocatalytic Phosphine‐Mediated Deoxygenative Coupling of Aspartic/Glutamic Acid Residues and Azobenzenes to Access Hydrazide‐Containing Unnatural Amino Acids
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Herein, we have developed a novel synergistic catalysis strategy combining visible‐light photocatalysis with triphenylphosphine, enabling efficient and highly selective deoxygenative coupling of aspartic/glutamic acid derivatives with azobenzene. This approach provides a general and versatile method for synthesizing a series of pharmacologically interesting hydrazide‐containing unnatural amino acids.
Herein, we have developed a novel synergistic catalysis strategy combining visible‐light photocatalysis with triphenylphosphine, enabling efficient and highly selective deoxygenative coupling of aspartic/glutamic acid derivatives with azobenzene. This approach provides a general and versatile method for synthesizing a series of pharmacologically interesting hydrazide‐containing unnatural amino acids. Mechanistic studies confirmed a radical‐based pathway and elucidated the unique role of DMAP in modulating the reaction pathway through stabilization of the critical intermediate. This approach provides a new type of amino acids residues with robust active site, which are significant organic building blocks and potential bioactivity.
item.content:encoded:
Herein, we have developed a novel synergistic catalysis strategy combining visible-light photocatalysis with triphenylphosphine, enabling efficient and highly selective deoxygenative coupling of aspartic/glutamic acid derivatives with azobenzene. This approach provides a general and versatile method for synthesizing a series of pharmacologically interesting hydrazide-containing unnatural amino acids.


Herein, we have developed a novel synergistic catalysis strategy combining visible-light photocatalysis with triphenylphosphine, enabling efficient and highly selective deoxygenative coupling of aspartic/glutamic acid derivatives with azobenzene. This approach provides a general and versatile method for synthesizing a series of pharmacologically interesting hydrazide-containing unnatural amino acids. Mechanistic studies confirmed a radical-based pathway and elucidated the unique role of DMAP in modulating the reaction pathway through stabilization of the critical intermediate. This approach provides a new type of amino acids residues with robust active site, which are significant organic building blocks and potential bioactivity.

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Machine Learning‐Driven Isolated Yield Prediction and Experimental Parameter Optimization for Ferulic Acid Synthesis
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
This study developed a machine learning framework benchmarking ten algorithms for the high‐precision prediction and optimization of ferulic acid isolated yield. Shapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) analysis and Bi‐factor Partial Dependence Plots (Bi‐PDPs) were further implemented to identify critical governing factors and decode complex synergistic interactions, providing a theoretical foundation for rational experimental design.
Chemical synthesis is an essential method for obtaining high‐purity ferulic acid (FA). However, due to the inherent complexity of the Knoevenagel condensation system and the nonlinear coupling among process parameters, traditional approaches often fail to achieve precise isolated yield prediction and optimization. This study introduces a machine learning (ML)‐guided experimental optimization strategy. Ten representative algorithms, including Gradient Boosting Decision Trees (GBDT), Random Forest (RF), Extreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost), Support Vector Regression (SVR), Adaptive Boosting (AdaBoost), Categorical Boosting (CatBoost), k‐Nearest Neighbors (KNN), Linear Regression, Decision Trees (DT), and Ridge, were constructed and systematically evaluated. The results indicate that the RF model exhibits superior fitting accuracy and exhibits potential for generalization. Based on the RF model, Shapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) and Bi‐factor Partial Dependence Plots (Bi‐PDPs) were employed to quantitatively assess the influence of individual factors and to elucidate interactive effects between variables. This work establishes an efficient ML‐driven framework for the rational optimization of complex organic synthesis processes.
item.content:encoded:
This study developed a machine learning framework benchmarking ten algorithms for the high-precision prediction and optimization of ferulic acid isolated yield. Shapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) analysis and Bi-factor Partial Dependence Plots (Bi-PDPs) were further implemented to identify critical governing factors and decode complex synergistic interactions, providing a theoretical foundation for rational experimental design.


Chemical synthesis is an essential method for obtaining high-purity ferulic acid (FA). However, due to the inherent complexity of the Knoevenagel condensation system and the nonlinear coupling among process parameters, traditional approaches often fail to achieve precise isolated yield prediction and optimization. This study introduces a machine learning (ML)-guided experimental optimization strategy. Ten representative algorithms, including Gradient Boosting Decision Trees (GBDT), Random Forest (RF), Extreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost), Support Vector Regression (SVR), Adaptive Boosting (AdaBoost), Categorical Boosting (CatBoost), k-Nearest Neighbors (KNN), Linear Regression, Decision Trees (DT), and Ridge, were constructed and systematically evaluated. The results indicate that the RF model exhibits superior fitting accuracy and exhibits potential for generalization. Based on the RF model, Shapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) and Bi-factor Partial Dependence Plots (Bi-PDPs) were employed to quantitatively assess the influence of individual factors and to elucidate interactive effects between variables. This work establishes an efficient ML-driven framework for the rational optimization of complex organic synthesis processes.

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Diboron‐Mediated Chemoselective Radical Reduction of 4‐Acylpyridines to Alcohols Under Metal‐Free Aqueous Conditions
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A transition metal‐free protocol for the reduction of 4‐acylpyridines is developed using diborons in water. Acting as a green surrogate for classical single‐electron transfer (SET) reagents like SmI2, this system demonstrates high chemoselectivity. It reduces the carbonyl group via a radical pathway while preserving the pyridine core intact.
Radical reduction of carbonyl compounds is a powerful transformation typically achieved using single‐electron transfer (SET) reagents such as samarium diiodide (SmI2) or alkali metals. While effective, these methods often require strictly anhydrous conditions, inert atmospheres, or expensive reagents. Herein, we report a robust, transition metal‐free protocol for the radical‐type reduction of 4‐acylpyridines to secondary alcohols using diboron reagents in water. This system serves as an air‐stable surrogate for classical SET reductants. The diboron/water system operates under mild conditions, ensuring high chemoselectivity toward the exocyclic carbonyl group while preserving the heteroaromatic core. Preliminary mechanistic investigations support a radical pathway, highlighting the capability of diboron reagents to function as effective radical reductants in aqueous media.
item.content:encoded:
A transition metal-free protocol for the reduction of 4-acylpyridines is developed using diborons in water. Acting as a green surrogate for classical single-electron transfer (SET) reagents like SmI2, this system demonstrates high chemoselectivity. It reduces the carbonyl group via a radical pathway while preserving the pyridine core intact.


Radical reduction of carbonyl compounds is a powerful transformation typically achieved using single-electron transfer (SET) reagents such as samarium diiodide (SmI2) or alkali metals. While effective, these methods often require strictly anhydrous conditions, inert atmospheres, or expensive reagents. Herein, we report a robust, transition metal-free protocol for the radical-type reduction of 4-acylpyridines to secondary alcohols using diboron reagents in water. This system serves as an air-stable surrogate for classical SET reductants. The diboron/water system operates under mild conditions, ensuring high chemoselectivity toward the exocyclic carbonyl group while preserving the heteroaromatic core. Preliminary mechanistic investigations support a radical pathway, highlighting the capability of diboron reagents to function as effective radical reductants in aqueous media.

item.dc:title: Diboron‐Mediated Chemoselective Radical Reduction of 4‐Acylpyridines to Alcohols Under Metal‐Free Aqueous Conditions

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Mechanochemical Synthesis of Cysteine‐Containing Peptides via Native Chemical Ligation
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Cysteine‐containing peptides were efficiently synthesized through mechanochemical native chemical ligation.
Cysteine‐containing peptides are important building blocks for the preparation of artificially designed peptides. Therefore, the development of efficient methods for the synthesis of cysteine‐containing peptides is recognized as an important area of research. To achieve an efficient preparation method for cysteine‐containing peptides, we became interested in developing a mechanochemical native chemical ligation approach. First, amino acid thioesters, which are key reagents for native chemical ligation, were prepared in a ball‐mill reactor using a user‐friendly, odorless thiol reagent. Next, mechanochemical native chemical ligation between amino acid thioesters and cysteine derivatives was performed. As a result of these mechanochemical processes, cysteine‐containing peptides, including C‐terminal unprotected peptides, were efficiently prepared.
item.content:encoded:
Cysteine-containing peptides were efficiently synthesized through mechanochemical native chemical ligation.


Cysteine-containing peptides are important building blocks for the preparation of artificially designed peptides. Therefore, the development of efficient methods for the synthesis of cysteine-containing peptides is recognized as an important area of research. To achieve an efficient preparation method for cysteine-containing peptides, we became interested in developing a mechanochemical native chemical ligation approach. First, amino acid thioesters, which are key reagents for native chemical ligation, were prepared in a ball-mill reactor using a user-friendly, odorless thiol reagent. Next, mechanochemical native chemical ligation between amino acid thioesters and cysteine derivatives was performed. As a result of these mechanochemical processes, cysteine-containing peptides, including C-terminal unprotected peptides, were efficiently prepared.
item.dc:creator: Sao Sumida, Yuki Kinoshita, Taiki Mori, Seiji Shirakawa
item.dc:title: Mechanochemical Synthesis of Cysteine‐Containing Peptides via Native Chemical Ligation

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Iodine (III)‐Promoted Radical Alkenylation of Cyclic Ethers
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A green and sustainable oxidative alkenylation of simple ethers to construct allylic ethers was achieved using iodine(III) as a feasible dehydrogenation agent under KBr. Notably, open‐chain ethers were also found to be efficient coupling partners in this reaction, and this reaction is likely to proceed via a radical process.
A sustainable oxidative alkenylation of simple ethers to construct allylic ethers was achieved using iodine (III) as a feasible dehydrogenation agent in the presence of KBr. Notably, open‐chain ethers were also found to be efficient coupling partners in this reaction and are likely to proceed via a radical process. Hydrogen atom transfer from ethers to radicals is supported by both experimental and computational studies. This reaction provides good yields and broad functional group compatibility, along with simple operation and mild conditions.
