Notably, open-chain ethers were also found to be efficient coupling partners in this reaction, and this reaction is likely to proceed via a radical process.


A sustainable oxidative alkenylation of simple ethers to construct allylic ethers was achieved using iodine (III) as a feasible dehydrogenation agent in the presence of KBr. Notably, open-chain ethers were also found to be efficient coupling partners in this reaction and are likely to proceed via a radical process. Hydrogen atom transfer from ethers to radicals is supported by both experimental and computational studies. This reaction provides good yields and broad functional group compatibility, along with simple operation and mild conditions.

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Dibenzosuberene‐Winged Polycyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbons
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
The twisted acene 8, which features two dibenzosuberene “wings”, was prepared by the addition of a crowded aryne to a similarly “winged” cyclopentadienone. Compound 8 is chiral, and it has a high barrier to thermal racemization (ΔG‡rac = 36 kcal/mol), but it undergoes fast photochemical racemization even in ambient light.
Two very crowded, twisted acenes with dibenzosuberene “wings”, 19,20,21,22‐tetraphenyl‐5H,14H‐bisdibenzo[3,4:6,7]cyclohepta[1,2‐a:1′, 2′‐c]naphthalene (7) and 19,28‐Diphenyl‐5H,14H‐bisdibenzo[3,4:6,7]cyclohepta[1,2‐a:1′, 2′‐c]dibenzo[h,j]anthracene (8), were prepared by addition of crowded arynes to the known bis(dibenzosubereno)cyclopentadienone 6. Their X‐ray structures showed the naphthalene and anthracene cores to have end‐to‐end twists of 32° and 67°, respectively. Compound 8 crystallized as a chiral conglomerate; thus, individual crystals contained only one enantiomer. The rate of racemization of 8 in solutions made from individual crystals was monitored by circular dichroism spectroscopy. Thermal racemization in the dark has a high barrier (t1/2 = 6.5 h at 450 K; ΔG‡rac = 36 kcal/mol), but exposure to even ambient light yields rapid racemization at room temperature. Computational studies of possible mechanisms of racemization of 7 and 8 are also reported.
item.content:encoded:
The twisted acene 8, which features two dibenzosuberene “wings”, was prepared by the addition of a crowded aryne to a similarly “winged” cyclopentadienone. Compound 8 is chiral, and it has a high barrier to thermal racemization (ΔG ‡ rac = 36 kcal/mol), but it undergoes fast photochemical racemization even in ambient light.


Two very crowded, twisted acenes with dibenzosuberene “wings”, 19,20,21,22-tetraphenyl-5H,14H-bisdibenzo[3,4:6,7]cyclohepta[1,2-a:1′, 2′-c]naphthalene (7) and 19,28-Diphenyl-5H,14H-bisdibenzo[3,4:6,7]cyclohepta[1,2-a:1′, 2′-c]dibenzo[h,j]anthracene (8), were prepared by addition of crowded arynes to the known bis(dibenzosubereno)cyclopentadienone 6. Their X-ray structures showed the naphthalene and anthracene cores to have end-to-end twists of 32° and 67°, respectively. Compound 8 crystallized as a chiral conglomerate; thus, individual crystals contained only one enantiomer. The rate of racemization of 8 in solutions made from individual crystals was monitored by circular dichroism spectroscopy. Thermal racemization in the dark has a high barrier (t 1/2 = 6.5 h at 450 K; ΔG ‡ rac = 36 kcal/mol), but exposure to even ambient light yields rapid racemization at room temperature. Computational studies of possible mechanisms of racemization of 7 and 8 are also reported.
item.dc:creator: Mingshu Xie, Xiaodong Zhang, Michael W. Johnson, Robert A. Pascal Jr
item.dc:title: Dibenzosuberene‐Winged Polycyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbons

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Palladium‐Catalyzed Transfer Hydrocarbonylation of Alkenes and Alkynes with Formic Acid Derivatives
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
This review focuses on developments in palladium‐catalyzed transfer hydrocarbonylation of unsaturated hydrocarbons. The content is organized according to the nucleophiles involved, covering four key reaction types: transfer hydroesterification, hydroaminocarbonylation, hydrothiocarbonylation, and hydrocarboxylation.
Transition‐metal‐catalyzed hydrocarbonylation constitutes a fundamental method for constructing carbonyl‐containing compounds with 100% atom economy. Despite its industrial relevance, the traditional hydrocarbonylation reaction employing pressurized CO has limited applicability in laboratory‐scale synthesis. To address this limitation, transfer hydrocarbonylation has emerged as a distinct alternative. This approach has circumvented the need for both external gaseous CO and separate nucleophiles by employing integrated transfer reagents that supply all requisite components. As a result, it has achieved high atom economy while offering enhanced operational simplicity under mild conditions. This review provides a systematic overview of palladium‐catalyzed transfer hydrocarbonylation of alkenes and alkynes. The discussion encompasses transfer hydroesterification, hydroaminocarbonylation, hydrothiocarbonylation, and hydrocarboxylation, with a particular focus on substrate scope, regioselectivities, and mechanistic insights. By integrating these developments, we provide a forward‐looking perspective on the role of this method in advancing valuable and practical synthesis of carbonyl compounds.
item.content:encoded:
This review focuses on developments in palladium-catalyzed transfer hydrocarbonylation of unsaturated hydrocarbons. The content is organized according to the nucleophiles involved, covering four key reaction types: transfer hydroesterification, hydroaminocarbonylation, hydrothiocarbonylation, and hydrocarboxylation.


Transition-metal-catalyzed hydrocarbonylation constitutes a fundamental method for constructing carbonyl-containing compounds with 100% atom economy. Despite its industrial relevance, the traditional hydrocarbonylation reaction employing pressurized CO has limited applicability in laboratory-scale synthesis. To address this limitation, transfer hydrocarbonylation has emerged as a distinct alternative. This approach has circumvented the need for both external gaseous CO and separate nucleophiles by employing integrated transfer reagents that supply all requisite components. As a result, it has achieved high atom economy while offering enhanced operational simplicity under mild conditions. This review provides a systematic overview of palladium-catalyzed transfer hydrocarbonylation of alkenes and alkynes. The discussion encompasses transfer hydroesterification, hydroaminocarbonylation, hydrothiocarbonylation, and hydrocarboxylation, with a particular focus on substrate scope, regioselectivities, and mechanistic insights. By integrating these developments, we provide a forward-looking perspective on the role of this method in advancing valuable and practical synthesis of carbonyl compounds.

item.dc:title: Palladium‐Catalyzed Transfer Hydrocarbonylation of Alkenes and Alkynes with Formic Acid Derivatives

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Electrochemical Halocyclization‐Enabled Synthesis of Tetrahydrofuran Derivatives With KBr/ KI
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A mild electrochemical strategy has been developed for synthesizing multisubstituted tetrahydrofuran derivatives via 1,5‐halocyclization of unactivated alkenols. This method employs readily available KBr/ KI as both the halogen source and supporting electrolyte, requiring no external oxidants or metal catalysts and producing H2 as the sole byproduct. This oxidant‐ and catalyst‐free protocol provides an efficient, atom‐economical route to synthetically important tetrahydrofuran (THF) scaffolds.
Tetrahydrofuran (THF) frameworks are privileged scaffolds prevalent in bioactive natural products and pharmaceuticals, yet the stereocontrolled synthesis of polysubstituted derivatives remains a significant challenge. While conventional methods often rely on transition‐metal catalysis, they are frequently hampered by issues such as β‐hydride elimination and the requirement for stoichiometric oxidants. In contrast, organic electrosynthesis offers a sustainable alternative by using electron transfer to activate substrates, eliminating the need for external oxidants and improving atom economy. Building on recent advances in electrochemical halogenation, this method proposes a novel and efficient strategy for constructing THF rings. We envisioned an electrochemical 1,5‐halocyclization of alkenols using inexpensive metal halides as dual‐function halogen sources and electrolytes. This anode‐driven process enables rapid assembly of the THF core, producing molecular hydrogen as the sole byproduct. This method represents a green, atom‐economical, and operationally simple approach to valuable O‐heterocycles.
item.content:encoded:
A mild electrochemical strategy has been developed for synthesizing multisubstituted tetrahydrofuran derivatives via 1,5-halocyclization of unactivated alkenols. This method employs readily available KBr/ KI as both the halogen source and supporting electrolyte, requiring no external oxidants or metal catalysts and producing H2 as the sole byproduct. This oxidant- and catalyst-free protocol provides an efficient, atom-economical route to synthetically important tetrahydrofuran (THF) scaffolds.


Tetrahydrofuran (THF) frameworks are privileged scaffolds prevalent in bioactive natural products and pharmaceuticals, yet the stereocontrolled synthesis of polysubstituted derivatives remains a significant challenge. While conventional methods often rely on transition-metal catalysis, they are frequently hampered by issues such as β-hydride elimination and the requirement for stoichiometric oxidants. In contrast, organic electrosynthesis offers a sustainable alternative by using electron transfer to activate substrates, eliminating the need for external oxidants and improving atom economy. Building on recent advances in electrochemical halogenation, this method proposes a novel and efficient strategy for constructing THF rings. We envisioned an electrochemical 1,5-halocyclization of alkenols using inexpensive metal halides as dual-function halogen sources and electrolytes. This anode-driven process enables rapid assembly of the THF core, producing molecular hydrogen as the sole byproduct. This method represents a green, atom-economical, and operationally simple approach to valuable O-heterocycles.
item.dc:creator: Zhenlei Zou, Jie Dong, Hui Wang, Tianle Xia, Zhaopan Yang, Weigang Zhang, Yi Wang
item.dc:title: Electrochemical Halocyclization‐Enabled Synthesis of Tetrahydrofuran Derivatives With KBr/ KI

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Recent Advances in Transition‐Metal‐ and External Photocatalyst‐Free Photochemical Reactions
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
This review summarizes recent advances in photochemical reactions that proceed without transition metals or external photocatalysts. By employing either direct excitation of substrates or photoexcitation of in situ formed electron donor–acceptor complexes, these transformations proceed via energy‐transfer, single‐electron transfer, or hydrogen atom transfer pathways, enabling efficient construction of C–C and C–heteroatom bonds under mild conditions.
In recent years, transition‐metal‐ and external photocatalyst‐free photochemical reactions have garnered significant attention as environmentally friendly and sustainable approaches to organic synthesis. These reactions rely on either direct photoexcitation of substrates or photoexcitation of electron donor–acceptor complexes to enable diverse transformations. This review summarizes representative advances reported in the recent years, organized by the construction of C–C and C–heteroatom bonds, and highlights key mechanistic features. This review aims to provide guidance and inspiration for reaction design and mechanistic exploration in this field.
item.content:encoded:
This review summarizes recent advances in photochemical reactions that proceed without transition metals or external photocatalysts. By employing either direct excitation of substrates or photoexcitation of in situ formed electron donor–acceptor complexes, these transformations proceed via energy-transfer, single-electron transfer, or hydrogen atom transfer pathways, enabling efficient construction of C–C and C–heteroatom bonds under mild conditions.


In recent years, transition-metal- and external photocatalyst-free photochemical reactions have garnered significant attention as environmentally friendly and sustainable approaches to organic synthesis. These reactions rely on either direct photoexcitation of substrates or photoexcitation of electron donor–acceptor complexes to enable diverse transformations. This review summarizes representative advances reported in the recent years, organized by the construction of C–C and C–heteroatom bonds, and highlights key mechanistic features. This review aims to provide guidance and inspiration for reaction design and mechanistic exploration in this field.

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Synthesis of Novel C‐Acyl‐Glycosyl Sulfonamides via Pd‐Catalyzed Carbonylative Coupling
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A Pd‐catalyzed carbonylative coupling of 1‐iodo‐glycals with diverse sulfonamides using Mo(CO)6 enables efficient access to C‐acyl‐glycosyl sulfonamides.This robust and versatile strategy delivers novel glycoside derivatives in good to excellent yields, highlighting their potential for drug discovery.
We developed an efficient methodology for synthesizing C‐acyl‐glycosyl sulfonamides via a carbonylative coupling reaction, using 1‐iodo‐glycals as substrates. A wide range of aromatic, heteroaromatic, and aliphatic sulfonamides was explored, employing Mo(CO)6 as a safe, solid source of carbon monoxide gas. This approach enabled the preparation of novel glycosyl sulfonamides featuring a C‐acyl‐linked framework under mild reaction conditions, achieving good to excellent yields.
item.content:encoded:
A Pd-catalyzed carbonylative coupling of 1-iodo-glycals with diverse sulfonamides using Mo(CO)6 enables efficient access to C-acyl-glycosyl sulfonamides.This robust and versatile strategy delivers novel glycoside derivatives in good to excellent yields, highlighting their potential for drug discovery.


We developed an efficient methodology for synthesizing C-acyl-glycosyl sulfonamides via a carbonylative coupling reaction, using 1-iodo-glycals as substrates. A wide range of aromatic, heteroaromatic, and aliphatic sulfonamides was explored, employing Mo(CO)6 as a safe, solid source of carbon monoxide gas. This approach enabled the preparation of novel glycosyl sulfonamides featuring a C-acyl-linked framework under mild reaction conditions, achieving good to excellent yields.
item.dc:creator: João A. F. Silva, Mônica F. Z. J. Toledo, Flávia Manarin, João H. de Araujo‐Neto, Milene M. Hornink, Hélio A. Stefani
item.dc:title: Synthesis of Novel C‐Acyl‐Glycosyl Sulfonamides via Pd‐Catalyzed Carbonylative Coupling

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Recent Advances in the Synthesis of Chromeno[4,3‐b]pyrrolidines
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Chromeno[4,3‐b]pyrrolidine derivatives are a class of compounds featuring a unique tricyclic fused scaffold and exhibiting significant biological activities. This review systematically summarizes the cyclization reactions catalyzed by various catalysts, all of which are employed for the construction of the Chromeno[4,3‐b]pyrrolidine scaffold.
Chromeno[4,3‐b]pyrrolidine derivatives are a class of compounds featuring a unique tricyclic fused scaffold and exhibiting significant biological activities. In recent years, remarkable progress has been made in developing efficient synthetic strategies for these complex structures. This review aims to systematically summarize various methods for synthesizing chromeno[4,3‐b]pyrrolidine derivatives. Based on the type of catalyst employed, we have summarized and categorized four main catalytic strategies: Brønsted base catalysis, N‐heterocyclic carbene catalysis, phosphine catalysis, and metal catalysis. The cyclization strategies and their proposed reaction mechanisms are discussed in detail, and we believe this review will provide valuable inspiration for future innovative research in this field.
item.content:encoded:
Chromeno[4,3-b]pyrrolidine derivatives are a class of compounds featuring a unique tricyclic fused scaffold and exhibiting significant biological activities. This review systematically summarizes the cyclization reactions catalyzed by various catalysts, all of which are employed for the construction of the Chromeno[4,3-b]pyrrolidine scaffold.


Chromeno[4,3-b]pyrrolidine derivatives are a class of compounds featuring a unique tricyclic fused scaffold and exhibiting significant biological activities. In recent years, remarkable progress has been made in developing efficient synthetic strategies for these complex structures. This review aims to systematically summarize various methods for synthesizing chromeno[4,3-b]pyrrolidine derivatives. Based on the type of catalyst employed, we have summarized and categorized four main catalytic strategies: Brønsted base catalysis, N-heterocyclic carbene catalysis, phosphine catalysis, and metal catalysis. The cyclization strategies and their proposed reaction mechanisms are discussed in detail, and we believe this review will provide valuable inspiration for future innovative research in this field.

item.dc:title: Recent Advances in the Synthesis of Chromeno[4,3‐b]pyrrolidines

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Photoinduced Halogen Atom Transfer of Alkyl Halides With N‐Arylsulfenyl Phthalimides Toward Alkyl‐Aryl Sulfides
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
An operationally simple photocatalytic strategy has been developed for the direct activation of alkyl halides via halogen atom transfer, enabling their coupling with N‐arylsulfenyl phthalimides to afford alkyl‐aryl sulfides. This transition‐metal‐free protocol proceeds under mild conditions and demonstrates a broad substrate scope.
The sulfenylation of organohalides has become a powerful strategy for CS bond formation. However, methods for activating alkyl halides in this context remain scarce. Herein, we report a simple photocatalytic strategy that generates alkyl radicals from alkyl halides via halogen atom transfer. These radicals subsequently couple efficiently with N‐arylsulfenyl phthalimides to afford alkyl‐aryl sulfides. This approach is transition‐metal‐free, operates under mild conditions, and exhibits a broad substrate scope.
item.content:encoded:
An operationally simple photocatalytic strategy has been developed for the direct activation of alkyl halides via halogen atom transfer, enabling their coupling with N-arylsulfenyl phthalimides to afford alkyl-aryl sulfides. This transition-metal-free protocol proceeds under mild conditions and demonstrates a broad substrate scope.


The sulfenylation of organohalides has become a powerful strategy for CS bond formation. However, methods for activating alkyl halides in this context remain scarce. Herein, we report a simple photocatalytic strategy that generates alkyl radicals from alkyl halides via halogen atom transfer. These radicals subsequently couple efficiently with N-arylsulfenyl phthalimides to afford alkyl-aryl sulfides. This approach is transition-metal-free, operates under mild conditions, and exhibits a broad substrate scope.

item.dc:title: Photoinduced Halogen Atom Transfer of Alkyl Halides With N‐Arylsulfenyl Phthalimides Toward Alkyl‐Aryl Sulfides

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Peptide Bond Formation via Anhydride Exchange‐Mediated In Situ Generation of N‐Methylimidazolium Cation
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Herein, we report a novel peptide bond formation strategy relying on anhydride exchange, which enables the in situ generation of N‐methylimidazolium cations by using trimethylacetic anhydride and catalytic N‐methylimidazole.
Herein, we report a method for peptide bond formation via anhydride exchange‐mediated in situ generation of N‐methylimidazolium cations using trimethylacetic anhydride (Piv2O) and catalytic N‐methylimidazole (NMI). It tolerates diverse amino acid side chains (e.g., −OH, indole NH) with no detectable racemization/epimerization even when dipeptides serve as electrophiles. Convergent synthesis of protected Leu‐enkephalin underscores its utility, expanding the toolbox for peptide bond formation.
item.content:encoded:
Herein, we report a novel peptide bond formation strategy relying on anhydride exchange, which enables the in situ generation of N-methylimidazolium cations by using trimethylacetic anhydride and catalytic N-methylimidazole.


Herein, we report a method for peptide bond formation via anhydride exchange-mediated in situ generation of N-methylimidazolium cations using trimethylacetic anhydride (Piv2O) and catalytic N-methylimidazole (NMI). It tolerates diverse amino acid side chains (e.g., −OH, indole NH) with no detectable racemization/epimerization even when dipeptides serve as electrophiles. Convergent synthesis of protected Leu-enkephalin underscores its utility, expanding the toolbox for peptide bond formation.
item.dc:creator: Chenchen Qi, Lulu Liu, Xiangsheng Xu, Xiaoqing Li
item.dc:title: Peptide Bond Formation via Anhydride Exchange‐Mediated In Situ Generation of N‐Methylimidazolium Cation

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Sustainable Synthesis of 2‐(Arylamino)nicotinic Acids Using Concentrated Solar Radiation
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A sustainable synthesis of 2‐(arylamino)nicotinic acids is achieved using concentrated solar radiation under catalyst‐ and solvent‐free conditions. This green method provides excellent yields of pharmaceutically relevant compounds in minutes. The protocol is successfully demonstrated on a gram scale for drugs like flunixin and clonixin, offering a rapid, energy‐efficient, and environmentally benign alternative to conventional synthetic routes.
2‐(Arylamino)nicotinic acids are significant in synthetic chemistry, particularly due to their applications in the pharmaceutical industry. An environmentally sustainable methodology has been developed for synthesizing these compounds, employing concentrated solar radiation (CSR) as a renewable energy source under catalyst‐ and solvent‐free conditions. This approach facilitates the rapid and selective synthesis of these biologically important compounds, achieving yields of up to 94% under mild conditions. Moreover, this method's synthetic applicability is demonstrated through synthesizing pharmaceutical compounds such as flunixin and clonixin on a gram scale. This eco‐friendly approach offers a promising alternative to traditional synthesis techniques and emphasizes the potential of CSR in promoting sustainable chemical processes.
item.content:encoded:
A sustainable synthesis of 2-(arylamino)nicotinic acids is achieved using concentrated solar radiation under catalyst- and solvent-free conditions. This green method provides excellent yields of pharmaceutically relevant compounds in minutes. The protocol is successfully demonstrated on a gram scale for drugs like flunixin and clonixin, offering a rapid, energy-efficient, and environmentally benign alternative to conventional synthetic routes.


2-(Arylamino)nicotinic acids are significant in synthetic chemistry, particularly due to their applications in the pharmaceutical industry. An environmentally sustainable methodology has been developed for synthesizing these compounds, employing concentrated solar radiation (CSR) as a renewable energy source under catalyst- and solvent-free conditions. This approach facilitates the rapid and selective synthesis of these biologically important compounds, achieving yields of up to 94% under mild conditions. Moreover, this method's synthetic applicability is demonstrated through synthesizing pharmaceutical compounds such as flunixin and clonixin on a gram scale. This eco-friendly approach offers a promising alternative to traditional synthesis techniques and emphasizes the potential of CSR in promoting sustainable chemical processes.

item.dc:title: Sustainable Synthesis of 2‐(Arylamino)nicotinic Acids Using Concentrated Solar Radiation

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Mechanochemical Hydrodeoxygenations
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A silver‐catalyzed mechanochemical hydrodeoxygenation with triethylsilane as reductant allows to convert ketones to the corresponding methylene derivatives in good yields after a short reaction time. Selected examples show that also alcohols, ethers, and sulfoxides can react.
In this work, a mechanochemical hydrodeoxygenation (HDO) was developed. Using silver triflate as catalyst and triethylsilane as reducing agent, 18 ketones and other substrates were hydrodeoxygenated in yields up to 92%.

item.dc:title: Mechanochemical Hydrodeoxygenations

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Rh(III)‐Catalyzed [4+2] Annulation of Imidazoles With α‐Diazocarbonyl Compounds: Direct Access to π‐Conjugated Imidazole‐Fused Isoquinolinone
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A Rh(III)‐catalyzed [4 + 2] annulation of 2‐aryl imidazoles/benzimidazoles with α‐diazocarbonyl compounds is developed for the synthesis of π‐conjugated imidazo/benzimidazo[2,1‐a]isoquinolinones systems. Mechanistic studies and control experiment reveal that the reaction proceeds via imidazole‐directed ortho C(sp2)–H activation, followed by carbene C–H insertion and subsequent intramolecular amidation to furnish the [4 + 2] annulation product.
A Rh(III)‐catalyzed [4 + 2] annulation of 2‐aryl imidazoles/benzimidazoles with α‐diazocarbonyl compounds has been developed for the synthesis of π‐conjugated imidazo/benzimidazo[2,1‐a]isoquinolinones systems. This method exhibits a broad substrate scope and excellent functional group tolerance, enabling the access of diverse tricyclic and tetracyclic π‐extended imidazole and benzimidazole derivatives. Mechanistic studies and control experiment revealed that the reaction proceeds via imidazole‐directed ortho C(sp2)–H activation, followed by carbene C–H insertion and subsequent intramolecular amidation to furnish the [4 + 2] annulation product. An optimized scaffold demonstrated potential relevance in materials and medicinal chemistry.
item.content:encoded:
A Rh(III)-catalyzed [4 + 2] annulation of 2-aryl imidazoles/benzimidazoles with α-diazocarbonyl compounds is developed for the synthesis of π-conjugated imidazo/benzimidazo[2,1-a]isoquinolinones systems. Mechanistic studies and control experiment reveal that the reaction proceeds via imidazole-directed ortho C(sp2)–H activation, followed by carbene C–H insertion and subsequent intramolecular amidation to furnish the [4 + 2] annulation product.


A Rh(III)-catalyzed [4 + 2] annulation of 2-aryl imidazoles/benzimidazoles with α-diazocarbonyl compounds has been developed for the synthesis of π-conjugated imidazo/benzimidazo[2,1-a]isoquinolinones systems. This method exhibits a broad substrate scope and excellent functional group tolerance, enabling the access of diverse tricyclic and tetracyclic π-extended imidazole and benzimidazole derivatives. Mechanistic studies and control experiment revealed that the reaction proceeds via imidazole-directed ortho C(sp2)–H activation, followed by carbene C–H insertion and subsequent intramolecular amidation to furnish the [4 + 2] annulation product. An optimized scaffold demonstrated potential relevance in materials and medicinal chemistry.
item.dc:creator: Ganesh P. Pawar, Duc Huy Le, Li Ching Shen, Sheng‐Cih Huang, Chung‐Ming Sun
item.dc:title: Rh(III)‐Catalyzed [4+2] Annulation of Imidazoles With α‐Diazocarbonyl Compounds: Direct Access to π‐Conjugated Imidazole‐Fused Isoquinolinone

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Pd‐Catalyzed Cyclotrimerization and Cycloaddition of 5,6‐Didehydrobenzo[8]annulenes (BenzoCOTynes): Formation of Nonplanar Tri[8]annulenes and Diels–Alder Cycloadducts
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A Pd(0)‐catalyzed cyclotrimerization of benzoCOTynes to form tri(benzo[8]annulenes) was developed. Three distinct isomers were isolated and structurally characterized by single‐crystal X‐ray diffraction. Ligand modulation proved crucial for enhancing regioselectivity: P(4‐OMePh), and (S)‐tBu‐Phox promoted the formation of the symmetric isomers. Both benzoCOTyne and benzodiCOTyne readily participated in Diels–Alder cycloadditions, affording the corresponding adducts in good yields.
We investigated the Pd(0)‐catalyzed cyclotrimerization of benzoCOTynes, which afforded a set of regioisomeric tri(benzo[8]annulene) products. Three distinct isomers were isolated and unambiguously characterized by single‐crystal X‐ray diffraction. Ligand modulation proved crucial for enhancing regioselectivity, with P(4‐OMePh)3 and (S)tBu‐Phox promoting the formation of the symmetric isomers. The resulting polycyclic frameworks exhibited high thermal stability, remaining intact up to 140°C. Furthermore, both benzoCOTyne and benzodiCOTyne readily participated in Diels–Alder cycloadditions, delivering the corresponding adducts in good yields.
item.content:encoded:
A Pd(0)-catalyzed cyclotrimerization of benzoCOTynes to form tri(benzo[8]annulenes) was developed. Three distinct isomers were isolated and structurally characterized by single-crystal X-ray diffraction. Ligand modulation proved crucial for enhancing regioselectivity: P(4-OMePh), and (S)-tBu-Phox promoted the formation of the symmetric isomers. Both benzoCOTyne and benzodiCOTyne readily participated in Diels–Alder cycloadditions, affording the corresponding adducts in good yields.


We investigated the Pd(0)-catalyzed cyclotrimerization of benzoCOTynes, which afforded a set of regioisomeric tri(benzo[8]annulene) products. Three distinct isomers were isolated and unambiguously characterized by single-crystal X-ray diffraction. Ligand modulation proved crucial for enhancing regioselectivity, with P(4-OMePh)3 and (S)tBu-Phox promoting the formation of the symmetric isomers. The resulting polycyclic frameworks exhibited high thermal stability, remaining intact up to 140°C. Furthermore, both benzoCOTyne and benzodiCOTyne readily participated in Diels–Alder cycloadditions, delivering the corresponding adducts in good yields.
item.dc:creator: Jesús Bello‐García, Jesús A. Varela, Carlos Saá
item.dc:title: Pd‐Catalyzed Cyclotrimerization and Cycloaddition of 5,6‐Didehydrobenzo[8]annulenes (BenzoCOTynes): Formation of Nonplanar Tri[8]annulenes and Diels–Alder Cycloadducts

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Recent Advances in Synthesis and Applications of Azoxybenzenes
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
The synthesis approaches to access azoxybenzenes employing thermal, photo‐redox mechanical and electrochemical conditions have been discussed. Their applications in key reactions including nitrene transfer reactions, azoxy group transfer, heterocyclic synthesis, and as directing groups in catalysis have been explored describing the recent trends and mechanistic interventions.
Azoxybenzene derivatives are important scaffolds in organic chemistry. These are widely used molecules having diverse bioactivity and several applications including fine chemicals synthesis, heterocyclic scaffolds synthesis, as directing groups in functionalization reactions and use as organic dyes. These are the vital intermediates observed during the redox pathways for the nitrobenzene to aniline conversions and vice‐versa. It is quite challenging to extract these intermediates as the final product from these redox pathways. Herein, we have focused on the synthesis approaches to access azoxybenzenes employing thermal, photo‐redox mechanical, and electrochemical conditions. Additionally, their applications in key reactions including nitrene transfer reactions, azoxy group transfer, heterocyclic synthesis, and as directing groups in catalysis have been explored describing the recent trends and mechanistic interventions.
item.content:encoded:
The synthesis approaches to access azoxybenzenes employing thermal, photo-redox mechanical and electrochemical conditions have been discussed. Their applications in key reactions including nitrene transfer reactions, azoxy group transfer, heterocyclic synthesis, and as directing groups in catalysis have been explored describing the recent trends and mechanistic interventions.


Azoxybenzene derivatives are important scaffolds in organic chemistry. These are widely used molecules having diverse bioactivity and several applications including fine chemicals synthesis, heterocyclic scaffolds synthesis, as directing groups in functionalization reactions and use as organic dyes. These are the vital intermediates observed during the redox pathways for the nitrobenzene to aniline conversions and vice-versa. It is quite challenging to extract these intermediates as the final product from these redox pathways. Herein, we have focused on the synthesis approaches to access azoxybenzenes employing thermal, photo-redox mechanical, and electrochemical conditions. Additionally, their applications in key reactions including nitrene transfer reactions, azoxy group transfer, heterocyclic synthesis, and as directing groups in catalysis have been explored describing the recent trends and mechanistic interventions.
item.dc:creator: Sandeep Kumar, Avinash Kumar, Aviti Sakshith, Ankush Gupta, Praveen Kumar Verma
item.dc:title: Recent Advances in Synthesis and Applications of Azoxybenzenes

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Metal‐Free [4‐OH‐TEMPO]+[NO3]−‐Catalyzed Aerobic Oxidation of Benzylic C(sp3)–H Bonds in Ethers
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A cheap organocatalyst, [4‐OH‐TEMPO]+[NO3]−, catalyzes the aerobic oxidation of benzylic C(sp3)H bonds in ethers under mild conditions. Hydride abstraction by the oxoammonium cation generates a stabilized carbocation intermediate, which undergoes nucleophilic attack by water to form the product. The hydroxylamine is regenerated to the oxoammonium cation via a NOx/O2 cycle.
A cheap organocatalyst, [4‐OH‐TEMPO]+[NO3]−, is presented for the aerobic oxidation of benzylic C(sp3)H bonds in ethers. The catalytic efficiency is attributed to the cooperative action of both the cation and anion moieties. The oxoammonium cation facilitates hydride abstraction from the C(sp3)H bond, generating a stabilized carbocation intermediate, which is subsequently attacked by water to form the CO bond. Meanwhile, the nitrate anion participates in a NOx/O2 cycle, regenerating the oxoammonium cation from its reduced hydroxylamine form. Under mild reaction conditions, this salt catalyzes the oxidation of ethers to produce lactones or ketones in excellent yields, even on a 10 mmol scale. Control experiments confirm that the carbocation intermediate is essential to the reaction and that the oxygen atom in the product is derived from water.
item.content:encoded:
A cheap organocatalyst, [4-OH-TEMPO]+[NO3]−, catalyzes the aerobic oxidation of benzylic C(sp3)H bonds in ethers under mild conditions. Hydride abstraction by the oxoammonium cation generates a stabilized carbocation intermediate, which undergoes nucleophilic attack by water to form the product. The hydroxylamine is regenerated to the oxoammonium cation via a NO x /O2 cycle.


A cheap organocatalyst, [4-OH-TEMPO]+[NO3]−, is presented for the aerobic oxidation of benzylic C(sp3)H bonds in ethers. The catalytic efficiency is attributed to the cooperative action of both the cation and anion moieties. The oxoammonium cation facilitates hydride abstraction from the C(sp3)H bond, generating a stabilized carbocation intermediate, which is subsequently attacked by water to form the CO bond. Meanwhile, the nitrate anion participates in a NO x /O2 cycle, regenerating the oxoammonium cation from its reduced hydroxylamine form. Under mild reaction conditions, this salt catalyzes the oxidation of ethers to produce lactones or ketones in excellent yields, even on a 10 mmol scale. Control experiments confirm that the carbocation intermediate is essential to the reaction and that the oxygen atom in the product is derived from water.
item.dc:creator: Junpeng Chen, Yongtao Wang, Wanqi Sun, Jia Yao, Haoran Li
item.dc:title: Metal‐Free [4‐OH‐TEMPO]+[NO3]−‐Catalyzed Aerobic Oxidation of Benzylic C(sp3)–H Bonds in Ethers

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Electrochemical Borylation of Aryldiazonium Salts: A Catalyst‐Free Approach to CB Bond Formation
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A novel electrochemical method enables the synthesis of arylboronates from aryldiazonium salts and B2pin2 in an undivided cell under constant‐current electrolysis, using electricity as the sole redox agent. The metal‐ and additive‐free protocol exhibits broad substrate scope and delivers products in moderate to good yields, with most cases exceeding 70%.
This study presents an electrochemical strategy for the synthesis of arylboronates from aryldiazonium salts and bis(pinacolato)diboron (B2pin2). The transformation is achieved via constant‐current electrolysis in an undivided cell, without requiring external oxidants or reductants, metal catalysts, acid/base additives, or any additional promoters. Notably, the method exhibits excellent functional‐group tolerance, accommodating a wide range of structurally diverse substrates and delivering the target arylboronates in moderate to good yields, with most exceeding 70%.
item.content:encoded:
A novel electrochemical method enables the synthesis of arylboronates from aryldiazonium salts and B2pin2 in an undivided cell under constant-current electrolysis, using electricity as the sole redox agent. The metal- and additive-free protocol exhibits broad substrate scope and delivers products in moderate to good yields, with most cases exceeding 70%.


This study presents an electrochemical strategy for the synthesis of arylboronates from aryldiazonium salts and bis(pinacolato)diboron (B2pin2). The transformation is achieved via constant-current electrolysis in an undivided cell, without requiring external oxidants or reductants, metal catalysts, acid/base additives, or any additional promoters. Notably, the method exhibits excellent functional-group tolerance, accommodating a wide range of structurally diverse substrates and delivering the target arylboronates in moderate to good yields, with most exceeding 70%.

item.dc:title: Electrochemical Borylation of Aryldiazonium Salts: A Catalyst‐Free Approach to CB Bond Formation

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: A Convenient Undivided Cell Strategy for the Electrochemical Gewald Reaction
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
In this work, we introduce a practical electrochemical Gewald protocol in a commercial, undivided cell reactor. Our optimized conditions enable the efficient synthesis of functionalized 2‐aminothiophenes, which are privileged scaffolds in medicinal chemistry, while accommodating complex substrates and drug molecules under mild, reproducible conditions.
The Gewald reaction is a key method for synthesizing 2‐aminothiophenes, which are valuable scaffolds in drug discovery. Here, we report a practical electrochemical approach to the Gewald reaction using an undivided cell setup in the IKA ElectraSyn 2.0 reactor. Key parameters, including electrode materials, electrolyte type, current, and electron equivalents, were systematically investigated to maximize yields. The optimized protocol demonstrated broad substrate scope, tolerating diverse functional groups and ring systems. Late‐stage functionalization and a one‐pot transformation were achieved on complex molecules, and the method was successfully applied to the synthesis of the non‐steroidal anti‐inflammatory drug tinoridine. This robust, scalable, and sustainable electrochemical strategy offers a convenient and complementary method to traditional Gewald reactions, expanding access to 2‐aminothiophenes for medicinal chemistry.
item.content:encoded:
In this work, we introduce a practical electrochemical Gewald protocol in a commercial, undivided cell reactor. Our optimized conditions enable the efficient synthesis of functionalized 2-aminothiophenes, which are privileged scaffolds in medicinal chemistry, while accommodating complex substrates and drug molecules under mild, reproducible conditions.


The Gewald reaction is a key method for synthesizing 2-aminothiophenes, which are valuable scaffolds in drug discovery. Here, we report a practical electrochemical approach to the Gewald reaction using an undivided cell setup in the IKA ElectraSyn 2.0 reactor. Key parameters, including electrode materials, electrolyte type, current, and electron equivalents, were systematically investigated to maximize yields. The optimized protocol demonstrated broad substrate scope, tolerating diverse functional groups and ring systems. Late-stage functionalization and a one-pot transformation were achieved on complex molecules, and the method was successfully applied to the synthesis of the non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drug tinoridine. This robust, scalable, and sustainable electrochemical strategy offers a convenient and complementary method to traditional Gewald reactions, expanding access to 2-aminothiophenes for medicinal chemistry.

item.dc:title: A Convenient Undivided Cell Strategy for the Electrochemical Gewald Reaction

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Recent Advances in [5 + 4] Cycloaddition Reactions: Catalysis, Mechanism, and Reaction Optimization
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
[5+4] cycloaddition emerges as a powerful yet underexplored strategy for constructing challenging nine‐membered rings. This review highlights advances from 2000 to 2025, showcasing how metal, organo‐, and photocatalysts overcome thermodynamic and transannular barriers, enabling efficient synthesis of medium‐sized scaffolds with significant biological relevance.
Medium‐sized rings, especially nine‐membered scaffolds, represent the core framework of several promising biologically active compounds. Among the various synthetic strategies reported, cycloaddition reactions, particularly [5 + 4] cycloaddition reactions, represent a unique and relatively underexplored class of pericyclic reactions that form the nine‐membered ring systems. However, the construction of a nine‐membered ring remains a significant challenge due to thermodynamic barriers, involving unfavorable enthalpic and entropic factors, as well as transannular interactions. Numerous catalytic systems have been developed to overcome these challenges. In this review article, we examined literature from 2000 to 2025 on [5 + 4] cycloaddition reaction, highlighting the role of various catalytic strategies such as metal catalysts, organocatalysts, and photocatalysts.
item.content:encoded:
[5+4] cycloaddition emerges as a powerful yet underexplored strategy for constructing challenging nine-membered rings. This review highlights advances from 2000 to 2025, showcasing how metal, organo-, and photocatalysts overcome thermodynamic and transannular barriers, enabling efficient synthesis of medium-sized scaffolds with significant biological relevance.


Medium-sized rings, especially nine-membered scaffolds, represent the core framework of several promising biologically active compounds. Among the various synthetic strategies reported, cycloaddition reactions, particularly [5 + 4] cycloaddition reactions, represent a unique and relatively underexplored class of pericyclic reactions that form the nine-membered ring systems. However, the construction of a nine-membered ring remains a significant challenge due to thermodynamic barriers, involving unfavorable enthalpic and entropic factors, as well as transannular interactions. Numerous catalytic systems have been developed to overcome these challenges. In this review article, we examined literature from 2000 to 2025 on [5 + 4] cycloaddition reaction, highlighting the role of various catalytic strategies such as metal catalysts, organocatalysts, and photocatalysts.

item.dc:title: Recent Advances in [5 + 4] Cycloaddition Reactions: Catalysis, Mechanism, and Reaction Optimization

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: N‐Glycosyl Triazoline Glycomimetics via Huisgen Cycloaddition With an Electron‐Deficient Alkene and BF3‐Catalysed Triazoline Fragmentation to Aziridines
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Glycomimetics with triazoline and aziridine groups were synthesised via diastereoselective Huisgen cycloaddition from glycosyl azides. Density functional theory was applied to define the mechanism for BF3 promoted aziridine formation.
Triazoline and aziridine derivatives were synthesised by diastereo‐ and regioselective Huisgen cycloaddition of 2,3,4,6‐tetra‐O‐acetyl‐β‐D‐galactopyranosyl azide with ethyl 4,4,4‐trifluorocrotonate. Use of a continuous‐flow reactor led to reduced reaction time and enabled use of ethyl acetate as a greener solvent for the cycloaddition. Thermolysis of trans‐triazolines yielded trans‐aziridines, while treatment with BF3·OEt2 (or BnOTf) produced cis‐aziridines. Density functional theory calculations supported a mechanism for trans‐triazoline conversion to cis‐aziridine involving boron trifluoride catalysed NN bond cleavage to give an intermediate with boron trifluoride stabilised nitrogen that then undergoes SN2 reaction. Deacetylation yielded N‐galactopyranosyl aziridines and triazolines, which were identified as substrate‐based inhibitors of β‐galactosidase. Synthesis of glycosyl aziridines from reactions of N‐phenylmaleimide is included.
item.content:encoded:
Glycomimetics with triazoline and aziridine groups were synthesised via diastereoselective Huisgen cycloaddition from glycosyl azides. Density functional theory was applied to define the mechanism for BF3 promoted aziridine formation.


Triazoline and aziridine derivatives were synthesised by diastereo- and regioselective Huisgen cycloaddition of 2,3,4,6-tetra-O-acetyl-β-D-galactopyranosyl azide with ethyl 4,4,4-trifluorocrotonate. Use of a continuous-flow reactor led to reduced reaction time and enabled use of ethyl acetate as a greener solvent for the cycloaddition. Thermolysis of trans-triazolines yielded trans-aziridines, while treatment with BF3·OEt2 (or BnOTf) produced cis-aziridines. Density functional theory calculations supported a mechanism for trans-triazoline conversion to cis-aziridine involving boron trifluoride catalysed NN bond cleavage to give an intermediate with boron trifluoride stabilised nitrogen that then undergoes SN2 reaction. Deacetylation yielded N-galactopyranosyl aziridines and triazolines, which were identified as substrate-based inhibitors of β-galactosidase. Synthesis of glycosyl aziridines from reactions of N-phenylmaleimide is included.
item.dc:creator: Aaron McCormack, Ashis Dhara, Lamis Alaa Eldin Refat, Mikael Bols, Paul V. Murphy
item.dc:title: N‐Glycosyl Triazoline Glycomimetics via Huisgen Cycloaddition With an Electron‐Deficient Alkene and BF3‐Catalysed Triazoline Fragmentation to Aziridines

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Synthesis of N–N Atropisomers via De Novo Construction of Nitrogen‐Containing Heterocycles
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
This review provides an in‐depth analysis of the state‐of‐the‐art techniques for catalytic asymmetric synthesis of N–N atropisomers via the de novo construction of nitrogen‐containing heterocycles, such as pyrroles, indoles, isoquinolones, benzimidazoles, isoindolinones, quinazolinones, dihydropyridinones, and pyrimidinones.
In recent years, the N–N atropisomers have drawn increasing attention compared to their well‐developed C–C and C–N analogs due to their potential application in biological and material chemistry. Since the first report in 2021, catalytic asymmetric synthesis of N–N atropisomers has become an emerging research topic, and strategies, including asymmetric N−H bond functionalization, asymmetric desymmetrization, and atroposelective de novo construction of heterocycles, are developed to offer this novel atropisomeric framework. In this review, we summarize the recent advancements in catalytic asymmetric synthesis of N–N atropisomers via de novo construction of nitrogen‐containing heterocycles, such as pyrroles, indoles, isoquinolones, benzimidazoles, isoindolinones, quinazolinones, dihydropyridinones, and pyrimidinones.
item.content:encoded:
This review provides an in-depth analysis of the state-of-the-art techniques for catalytic asymmetric synthesis of N–N atropisomers via the de novo construction of nitrogen-containing heterocycles, such as pyrroles, indoles, isoquinolones, benzimidazoles, isoindolinones, quinazolinones, dihydropyridinones, and pyrimidinones.


In recent years, the N–N atropisomers have drawn increasing attention compared to their well-developed C–C and C–N analogs due to their potential application in biological and material chemistry. Since the first report in 2021, catalytic asymmetric synthesis of N–N atropisomers has become an emerging research topic, and strategies, including asymmetric N−H bond functionalization, asymmetric desymmetrization, and atroposelective de novo construction of heterocycles, are developed to offer this novel atropisomeric framework. In this review, we summarize the recent advancements in catalytic asymmetric synthesis of N–N atropisomers via de novo construction of nitrogen-containing heterocycles, such as pyrroles, indoles, isoquinolones, benzimidazoles, isoindolinones, quinazolinones, dihydropyridinones, and pyrimidinones.

item.dc:title: Synthesis of N–N Atropisomers via De Novo Construction of Nitrogen‐Containing Heterocycles

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Ni‐Catalyzed Reductive Acyl Cross‐Coupling: Straightforward Synthesis of α‐Trifluoromethylthiolated Ketones
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
The trifluoromethylthiol group (‐SCF3) has attracted considerable attention due to its high lipophilicity and strong electron‐withdrawing ability. Over the past decades, numerous methodologies have been developed for the construction of C(sp2)–SCF3 bonds through the selective introduction of the SCF3 group into arenes or vinyl derivatives. In contrast, the synthesis of tertiary C(sp3)‐SCF3 compounds remains more restricted. Herein, we present a Ni‐catalyzed reductive acyl cross‐coupling that enables the direct construction of α‐trifluoromethylthiolated ketones in a single step utilizing a recently developed SCF3‐containing electrophiles. Moreover, mechanistic investigations have demonstrated the presence of an unusual pathway for reductive coupling and the essential role of zinc.

Over the past decades, numerous methodologies have been developed for the construction of C(sp2)–SCF3 bonds through the selective introduction of the SCF3 group into arenes or vinyl derivatives. In contrast, the synthesis of tertiary C(sp3)‐SCF3 compounds remains more restricted. Herein, we present a Ni‐catalyzed reductive acyl cross‐coupling that enables the direct construction of α‐trifluoromethylthiolated ketones in a single step utilizing a recently developed SCF3‐containing electrophiles. Moreover, mechanistic investigations have demonstrated the presence of an unusual pathway for reductive coupling and the essential role of zinc.

The trifluoromethylthiol group (-SCF3) has attracted considerable attention due to its high lipophilicity and strong electron-withdrawing ability. Over the past decades, numerous methodologies have been developed for the construction of C(sp2)–SCF3 bonds through the selective introduction of the SCF3 group into arenes or vinyl derivatives. In contrast, the synthesis of tertiary C(sp3)-SCF3 compounds remains more restricted. Herein, we present a Ni-catalyzed reductive acyl cross-coupling that enables the direct construction of α-trifluoromethylthiolated ketones in a single step utilizing a recently developed SCF3-containing electrophiles. Moreover, mechanistic investigations have demonstrated the presence of an unusual pathway for reductive coupling and the essential role of zinc.


The trifluoromethylthiol group (-SCF3) has attracted considerable attention due to its high lipophilicity and strong electron-withdrawing ability. Over the past decades, numerous methodologies have been developed for the construction of C(sp2)–SCF3 bonds through the selective introduction of the SCF3 group into arenes or vinyl derivatives. In contrast, the synthesis of tertiary C(sp3)-SCF3 compounds remains more restricted. Herein, we present a Ni-catalyzed reductive acyl cross-coupling that enables the direct construction of α-trifluoromethylthiolated ketones in a single step utilizing a recently developed SCF3-containing electrophiles. Moreover, mechanistic investigations have demonstrated the presence of an unusual pathway for reductive coupling and the essential role of zinc.
item.dc:creator: Xavier Vanderbiest, Olivier Riant
item.dc:title: Ni‐Catalyzed Reductive Acyl Cross‐Coupling: Straightforward Synthesis of α‐Trifluoromethylthiolated Ketones

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Emerging Trends in Organic Photoreactions Utilizing Disulfides
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Disulfides are versatile, nontoxic alternatives to metal photocatalysts. This review explores their role as photoactivated thiyl‐radical precursors and bifunctional catalysts (HAT/electron shuttling) in diverse transformations. Strategies for electronic tuning and development into recyclable heterogeneous materials are highlighted for sustainable, green photochemistry.
The facile photolysis of the sulfur‐sulfur bond in disulfides has emerged as a powerful and versatile strategy in organic chemistry. This review surveys the rapidly growing field of disulfide‐mediated photoreactions, including both catalytic and cocatalytic systems, as well as their roles across homogeneous and developing heterogeneous platforms. We highlight how electronically tuned disulfides function as photoactivated thiyl‐radical precursors and, in many cases, as bifunctional radical mediators that enable hydrogen‐atom transfer (HAT), electron shuttling, and polarity‐matched radical trapping. Representative reaction classes include anti‐Markovnikov hydrofunctionalizations of alkenes, oxidative alkene oxygenation and CC cleavage, hydrophosphinylation, hydrofluoroalkylation, decarboxylative transformations, C(sp3)–H functionalizations, and alkene/alkyne isomerizations. The review discusses the strategies for translating molecular disulfide chemistry into recyclable heterogeneous materials and polymer‐supported catalysts. The photochemical flexibility and tunability of disulfides through electronic modification, combined with their commercial availability and low toxicity, position them as attractive alternatives to expensive precious‐metal photoredox catalysts for advancing industrially viable and sustainable photochemistry.
item.content:encoded:
Disulfides are versatile, nontoxic alternatives to metal photocatalysts. This review explores their role as photoactivated thiyl-radical precursors and bifunctional catalysts (HAT/electron shuttling) in diverse transformations. Strategies for electronic tuning and development into recyclable heterogeneous materials are highlighted for sustainable, green photochemistry.


The facile photolysis of the sulfur-sulfur bond in disulfides has emerged as a powerful and versatile strategy in organic chemistry. This review surveys the rapidly growing field of disulfide-mediated photoreactions, including both catalytic and cocatalytic systems, as well as their roles across homogeneous and developing heterogeneous platforms. We highlight how electronically tuned disulfides function as photoactivated thiyl-radical precursors and, in many cases, as bifunctional radical mediators that enable hydrogen-atom transfer (HAT), electron shuttling, and polarity-matched radical trapping. Representative reaction classes include anti-Markovnikov hydrofunctionalizations of alkenes, oxidative alkene oxygenation and CC cleavage, hydrophosphinylation, hydrofluoroalkylation, decarboxylative transformations, C(sp3)–H functionalizations, and alkene/alkyne isomerizations. The review discusses the strategies for translating molecular disulfide chemistry into recyclable heterogeneous materials and polymer-supported catalysts. The photochemical flexibility and tunability of disulfides through electronic modification, combined with their commercial availability and low toxicity, position them as attractive alternatives to expensive precious-metal photoredox catalysts for advancing industrially viable and sustainable photochemistry.

item.dc:title: Emerging Trends in Organic Photoreactions Utilizing Disulfides

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Visible‐Light‐Induced Bisphosphonium Salt‐Catalyzed Facile Synthesis of Aldehydes and Ketones From Alkylarenes Under Air
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A metal‐free visible‐light photocatalytic protocol has been established for the oxidation of alkylarenes to aromatic aldehydes and ketones, using bisphosphonium salt as a photoredox catalyst and ambient air as the sole oxidant under mild conditions (395 nm LED, room temperature, MeCN). This additive‐free approach exhibits good functional group tolerance across diverse substrates, affording moderate to excellent yields, while circumventing the use of pure O2 to enhance operational safety and practicality.
Herein, we report an efficient method for the oxidation of simple alkylarenes to the corresponding aldehydes or ketones under visible light irradiation, using molecular oxygen from ambient air as the oxidant and bisphosphonium salt as the photoredox catalyst. This protocol obviates the need for additional metal catalysts or additives, thus emerging as a facile and green alternative for the synthesis of such carbonyl compounds. Preliminary substrate exploration demonstrates that this method is compatible with a broad range of functionalized substrates.
item.content:encoded:
A metal-free visible-light photocatalytic protocol has been established for the oxidation of alkylarenes to aromatic aldehydes and ketones, using bisphosphonium salt as a photoredox catalyst and ambient air as the sole oxidant under mild conditions (395 nm LED, room temperature, MeCN). This additive-free approach exhibits good functional group tolerance across diverse substrates, affording moderate to excellent yields, while circumventing the use of pure O2 to enhance operational safety and practicality.


Herein, we report an efficient method for the oxidation of simple alkylarenes to the corresponding aldehydes or ketones under visible light irradiation, using molecular oxygen from ambient air as the oxidant and bisphosphonium salt as the photoredox catalyst. This protocol obviates the need for additional metal catalysts or additives, thus emerging as a facile and green alternative for the synthesis of such carbonyl compounds. Preliminary substrate exploration demonstrates that this method is compatible with a broad range of functionalized substrates.
item.dc:creator: Zhuo Li, Lili Wang, Zheng Duan
item.dc:title: Visible‐Light‐Induced Bisphosphonium Salt‐Catalyzed Facile Synthesis of Aldehydes and Ketones From Alkylarenes Under Air

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Visible‐Light‐Driven Copper(II)‐Catalyzed Direct C–H Amination of Arenes via N‐Centered Radicals
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A visible‐light‐driven [Cu(dmbpy)(OAc)2] complex catalyzed direct C(sp2)–H amination of arenes is reported using N‐amino pyridinium salts as N‐centered radical precursors. This protocol proceeds under mild conditions, offering a broad substrate scope and successful late stage functionalization. Mechanistic studies support Cu(I) formation via visible‐light‐induced homolysis, as evidenced by UV–visible absorption and EPR spectroscopy.
The catalytic formation of C(sp2)N bonds through direct C–H functionalization remains a crucial method in the synthesis of aromatic amines. Herein, we report an efficient visible‐light‐driven, copper‐catalyzed direct C–H amination of arenes employing stable N‐amino pyridinium salts as versatile N‐centered radical precursors. The mild photocatalytic system generated from Cu(OAc)2 and the 6,6′‐dimethyl‐2,2′‐bipyridine ligand enables efficient CN bond formation under mild conditions and exhibits broad functional group compatibility across a diverse range of arenes. Mechanistic investigations, including UV–visible absorption and electron paramagnetic resonance studies, reveal the photoinduced generation of Cu(I) species from the [Cu(dmbpy)(OAc)2] complex via visible‐light‐induced homolysis. Moreover, successful late‐stage functionalization underscores the versatility of this visible‐light‐driven copper‐catalytic platform, enabling efficient access to aminated pharmaceutical analogs.
item.content:encoded:
A visible-light-driven [Cu(dmbpy)(OAc)2] complex catalyzed direct C(sp2)–H amination of arenes is reported using N-amino pyridinium salts as N-centered radical precursors. This protocol proceeds under mild conditions, offering a broad substrate scope and successful late stage functionalization. Mechanistic studies support Cu(I) formation via visible-light-induced homolysis, as evidenced by UV–visible absorption and EPR spectroscopy.


The catalytic formation of C(sp2)N bonds through direct C–H functionalization remains a crucial method in the synthesis of aromatic amines. Herein, we report an efficient visible-light-driven, copper-catalyzed direct C–H amination of arenes employing stable N-amino pyridinium salts as versatile N-centered radical precursors. The mild photocatalytic system generated from Cu(OAc)2 and the 6,6′-dimethyl-2,2′-bipyridine ligand enables efficient CN bond formation under mild conditions and exhibits broad functional group compatibility across a diverse range of arenes. Mechanistic investigations, including UV–visible absorption and electron paramagnetic resonance studies, reveal the photoinduced generation of Cu(I) species from the [Cu(dmbpy)(OAc)2] complex via visible-light-induced homolysis. Moreover, successful late-stage functionalization underscores the versatility of this visible-light-driven copper-catalytic platform, enabling efficient access to aminated pharmaceutical analogs.
item.dc:creator: Baldau Singh, Tiwari Neeraj Brijbhushan, Piyush Pandey, Abhijit Pandey, Dhandapani Ganapathy
item.dc:title: Visible‐Light‐Driven Copper(II)‐Catalyzed Direct C–H Amination of Arenes via N‐Centered Radicals

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Remarkable Formation of Indene Derivatives Upon Friedel–Crafts Acylation Reactions
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
This shows indene derivatives. Under the reaction conditions of a Friedel–Crafts acylation, alkyl benzenes underwent an unprecedented cyclopentannulation furnishing indene derivatives.
Alkylbenzene derivatives react with two equivalents of pivaloyl chloride (PivCl) in the presence of aluminum trichloride under formation of indene derivatives with up to 92% yield. Monosubstituted benzenes undergo this cyclopentannulation reaction with quantitative regioselectivity, which was proved by X‐ray single crystal structure analysis. A mechanistic proposal is based on the in situ‐formation of 2,2,5‐trimethyl‐4‐hexene‐3‐one from the acid chloride. This enone forms the indene derivatives in a sequence of two Friedel–Crafts alkylation reactions. Indeed, the 2,2,5‐trimethyl‐4‐hexene‐3‐one can be prepared from PivCl and AlCl3 in 68% yield. It can then be directly converted to the indene derivatives with yields up to 99%.
item.content:encoded:
This shows indene derivatives. Under the reaction conditions of a Friedel–Crafts acylation, alkyl benzenes underwent an unprecedented cyclopentannulation furnishing indene derivatives.


Alkylbenzene derivatives react with two equivalents of pivaloyl chloride (PivCl) in the presence of aluminum trichloride under formation of indene derivatives with up to 92% yield. Monosubstituted benzenes undergo this cyclopentannulation reaction with quantitative regioselectivity, which was proved by X-ray single crystal structure analysis. A mechanistic proposal is based on the in situ-formation of 2,2,5-trimethyl-4-hexene-3-one from the acid chloride. This enone forms the indene derivatives in a sequence of two Friedel–Crafts alkylation reactions. Indeed, the 2,2,5-trimethyl-4-hexene-3-one can be prepared from PivCl and AlCl3 in 68% yield. It can then be directly converted to the indene derivatives with yields up to 99%.
item.dc:creator: Leah Bantel, Neels Kruse, Marc Schmidtmann, Jens Christoffers
item.dc:title: Remarkable Formation of Indene Derivatives Upon Friedel–Crafts Acylation Reactions

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Chemical Syntheses of 3′ and 5′ Phosphorylated Oligonucleotides: An Overview
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
This review provides an overview of the various chemical methods employed in the synthesis of oligonucleotides bearing a phosphate group at their 3′ or 5′ end. Notably, it presents modified phosphoramidites and supports developed for this purpose.
Oligonucleotides (ON) with a phosphate monoester group at their 3′ or 5′ end have applications in many fields. Initially, the reagents used for their synthesis were similar to those used for the introduction of internucleotidic phosphates. However, as nucleic acid chemistry evolved, demanding specifications (RNA compatibility, purification aid, simplicity of the deprotection reaction) led to the development of more and more sophisticated reagents. This review will present the evolution and the most recent methods of 3′ or 5′ terminal phosphorylation of oligonucleotides. It will also recall the criteria to be met in the quest for the ideal reagent.
item.content:encoded:
This review provides an overview of the various chemical methods employed in the synthesis of oligonucleotides bearing a phosphate group at their 3′ or 5′ end. Notably, it presents modified phosphoramidites and supports developed for this purpose.

Initially, the reagents used for their synthesis were similar to those used for the introduction of internucleotidic phosphates. However, as nucleic acid chemistry evolved, demanding specifications (RNA compatibility, purification aid, simplicity of the deprotection reaction) led to the development of more and more sophisticated reagents. This review will present the evolution and the most recent methods of 3′ or 5′ terminal phosphorylation of oligonucleotides. It will also recall the criteria to be met in the quest for the ideal reagent.

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Synthesizing Multisubstituted Cyclohexanes via Palladium‐Catalyzed [4+2] Cycloaddition
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Heavily substituted cyclohexanes are synthesized via palladium‐catalyzed [4+2] cycloaddition between methylene‐δ‐valerolactones and electron‐deficient trisubstituted olefins under mild reaction conditions, which provides an efficient method for the synthesis of multisubstituted cyclohexans from simple substrates.
Cyclohexanes bearing three contiguous stereocenters, two of which are all‐carbon quaternary centrers, are synthesized via palladium‐catalyzed [4+2] cycloaddition between methylene‐δ‐valerolactones and electron‐deficient trisubstituted olefins. The reaction proceeds under mild conditions and exhibits broad substrate scope. Preliminary studies using chiral ligands afforded the corresponding chiral adduct in good yield and moderate enantioselectivity.
item.content:encoded:
Heavily substituted cyclohexanes are synthesized via palladium-catalyzed [4+2] cycloaddition between methylene-δ-valerolactones and electron-deficient trisubstituted olefins under mild reaction conditions, which provides an efficient method for the synthesis of multisubstituted cyclohexans from simple substrates.


Cyclohexanes bearing three contiguous stereocenters, two of which are all-carbon quaternary centrers, are synthesized via palladium-catalyzed [4+2] cycloaddition between methylene-δ-valerolactones and electron-deficient trisubstituted olefins. The reaction proceeds under mild conditions and exhibits broad substrate scope. Preliminary studies using chiral ligands afforded the corresponding chiral adduct in good yield and moderate enantioselectivity.
item.dc:creator: Jiajun Mo, Xuyang Li, Jie Peng, Yungui Peng, Guihua Chen
item.dc:title: Synthesizing Multisubstituted Cyclohexanes via Palladium‐Catalyzed [4+2] Cycloaddition

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Synthesis and Physicochemical Properties of trans‐2‐Fluoroalkyl‐Substituted Cyclopentane Building Blocks
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A scalable approach to the multigram synthesis of trans−1,2‐fluoroalkyl‐substituted cyclopentane building blocks based on orthogonal functionalization of a common precursor is described. The effect of the fluoroalkyl substituents on the compound's physicochemical properties (pKa and LogP) was established for a series of model derivatives.
A scalable synthesis of trans−1,2‐fluoroalkyl‐substituted cyclopentane building blocks was developed. The approach relies on orthogonal late‐stage functionalization of a common precursor – monoester of trans−1,2‐cyclopentanecarboxylic acid. Fluoromethyl‐substituted derivatives were accessed via exhaustive reduction of the COOH group, activation of the resulting primary alcohol as a triflate, and nucleophilic substitution with fluoride. Meanwhile, difluoro‐ and trifluoromethyl groups were introduced through deoxofluorination of the corresponding aldehyde or carboxylic acid. The resulting fluorinated esters were efficiently diversified into a broad range of building blocks relevant to medicinal chemistry, including carboxylic acids, primary amines, azides, alcohols, and sulfonyl chlorides. In addition to the synthetic developments, the effect of the fluoroalkyl substituent (CH2F, CHF2, CF3) on physicochemical properties of the cyclopentane derivatives was established by measuring pKa and LogP values of model derivatives.
item.content:encoded:
A scalable approach to the multigram synthesis of trans−1,2-fluoroalkyl-substituted cyclopentane building blocks based on orthogonal functionalization of a common precursor is described. The effect of the fluoroalkyl substituents on the compound's physicochemical properties (pK a and LogP) was established for a series of model derivatives.


A scalable synthesis of trans−1,2-fluoroalkyl-substituted cyclopentane building blocks was developed. The approach relies on orthogonal late-stage functionalization of a common precursor – monoester of trans−1,2-cyclopentanecarboxylic acid. Fluoromethyl-substituted derivatives were accessed via exhaustive reduction of the COOH group, activation of the resulting primary alcohol as a triflate, and nucleophilic substitution with fluoride. Meanwhile, difluoro- and trifluoromethyl groups were introduced through deoxofluorination of the corresponding aldehyde or carboxylic acid. The resulting fluorinated esters were efficiently diversified into a broad range of building blocks relevant to medicinal chemistry, including carboxylic acids, primary amines, azides, alcohols, and sulfonyl chlorides. In addition to the synthetic developments, the effect of the fluoroalkyl substituent (CH2F, CHF2, CF3) on physicochemical properties of the cyclopentane derivatives was established by measuring pK a and LogP values of model derivatives.
