item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Radical Allylation and Benzylation of N‐Benzyl Ketimines with Allyl‐Bpins or 4‐Benzyl Hantzsch Esters Through Hydrogen Atom Transfer (HAT)/Photoredox Process
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A photoinduced radical coupling of ketimines and nucleophilic alkyl‐Bpins or 4‐benzyl Hantzsch esters without the requirement of a strong base is presented.
In the past decade, the functionalization of N‐benzyl ketimines has emerged as an attractive way toward the concise synthesis of α‐substituted benzyl amine derivatives. Besides the traditional nucleophilic substitution with 2‐azaallyl anions to alkyl halides, recent progress has focused on CC bond formation via 2‐azaallyl radicals. These processes require the use of a strong base to convert N‐benzyl ketimines to 2‐azaallyl anions, which perform as organic super‐electron‐donors, and only electrophiles can be used as the radical precursors for the coupling partners. Herein, we present the photoinduced benzylation and allylation of N‐benzyl ketimines with nucleophiles as the radical precursors, such as allyl Bpins (pinacol boronic esters) and 4‐benzyl Hantzsch esters. Lauroyl peroxide is employed to mediate 2‐azaallyl radical generation via a hydrogen atom transfer (HAT) process and furnish the redox cycle of the photocatalyst.
item.content:encoded:
A photoinduced radical coupling of ketimines and nucleophilic alkyl-Bpins or 4-benzyl Hantzsch esters without the requirement of a strong base is presented.


In the past decade, the functionalization of N-benzyl ketimines has emerged as an attractive way toward the concise synthesis of α-substituted benzyl amine derivatives. Besides the traditional nucleophilic substitution with 2-azaallyl anions to alkyl halides, recent progress has focused on CC bond formation via 2-azaallyl radicals. These processes require the use of a strong base to convert N-benzyl ketimines to 2-azaallyl anions, which perform as organic super-electron-donors, and only electrophiles can be used as the radical precursors for the coupling partners. Herein, we present the photoinduced benzylation and allylation of N-benzyl ketimines with nucleophiles as the radical precursors, such as allyl Bpins (pinacol boronic esters) and 4-benzyl Hantzsch esters. Lauroyl peroxide is employed to mediate 2-azaallyl radical generation via a hydrogen atom transfer (HAT) process and furnish the redox cycle of the photocatalyst.
item.dc:creator: Jia Liu, Fengxuan Chu, Xule Shen, Zuo‐Qin Liang, Lan‐Gui Xie
item.dc:title: Radical Allylation and Benzylation of N‐Benzyl Ketimines with Allyl‐Bpins or 4‐Benzyl Hantzsch Esters Through Hydrogen Atom Transfer (HAT)/Photoredox Process

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Site‐Selective C–H/C–H Cross Dehydrogenative Coupling Between Quinoxalin‐2(1H)‐Ones and Aliphatic Nitriles
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
An efficient and facile method was developed for the synthesis of C3‐cyanoalkylated quinoxalin‐2(1H)‐ones via a C–H/C–H cross dehydrogenation coupling between quinoxalin‐2(1H)‐ones and aliphatic nitriles.
An efficient and facile method was developed for the synthesis of C3‐cyanoalkylated quinoxalin‐2(1H)‐ones via a C–H/C–H cross dehydrogenation coupling between quinoxalin‐2(1H)‐ones and aliphatic nitriles. The method enables the β‐ or γ‐site‐selective C−H activation of aliphatic nitriles, giving the corresponding products in moderated to good yields. The reaction demonstrated a broad substrate scope and useful synthetic application ability.
item.content:encoded:
An efficient and facile method was developed for the synthesis of C3-cyanoalkylated quinoxalin-2(1H)-ones via a C–H/C–H cross dehydrogenation coupling between quinoxalin-2(1H)-ones and aliphatic nitriles.


An efficient and facile method was developed for the synthesis of C3-cyanoalkylated quinoxalin-2(1H)-ones via a C–H/C–H cross dehydrogenation coupling between quinoxalin-2(1H)-ones and aliphatic nitriles. The method enables the β- or γ-site-selective C−H activation of aliphatic nitriles, giving the corresponding products in moderated to good yields. The reaction demonstrated a broad substrate scope and useful synthetic application ability.
item.dc:creator: Yizhuo Xue, Yingjie Zhang, Chuan Li, Zeguo Fang, Qian Zhang, Dong Li
item.dc:title: Site‐Selective C–H/C–H Cross Dehydrogenative Coupling Between Quinoxalin‐2(1H)‐Ones and Aliphatic Nitriles

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Gold‐Catalyzed Photoannulation: Access to Cyclic Scaffolds
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Merging of gold catalysis with photochemical activation has introduced synthetically valuable reactivity pattern and opened new avenues for the design of cyclic architectures. The present review shades light on recent advancements in gold‐enabled photocyclizations to build up diverse carbo‐ and heterocyclic scaffolds.
Gold catalysis has emerged as a revolutionary field in organic synthetic chemistry. The combination of gold catalysis with photochemical activation has further expanded this area, introducing synthetically valuable modes of reactivity. In fact, gold photocatalysis eliminates the need for stoichiometric sacrificial external oxidants and unlocks opportunitiesfor broader range of radical‐mediated transformations. In recent years, significant progress has been achieved in the development of gold‐catalyzed cyclization reactions facilitated by visible‐light irradiation. The present review highlights last 5 year advancements in gold‐enabled photocyclizations to build up diverse carbo‐ and heterocyclic scaffolds. This review offers comprehensive insights into mechanistic aspects and their significance in contemporary synthetic chemistry.
item.content:encoded:
Merging of gold catalysis with photochemical activation has introduced synthetically valuable reactivity pattern and opened new avenues for the design of cyclic architectures. The present review shades light on recent advancements in gold-enabled photocyclizations to build up diverse carbo- and heterocyclic scaffolds.


Gold catalysis has emerged as a revolutionary field in organic synthetic chemistry. The combination of gold catalysis with photochemical activation has further expanded this area, introducing synthetically valuable modes of reactivity. In fact, gold photocatalysis eliminates the need for stoichiometric sacrificial external oxidants and unlocks opportunitiesfor broader range of radical-mediated transformations. In recent years, significant progress has been achieved in the development of gold-catalyzed cyclization reactions facilitated by visible-light irradiation. The present review highlights last 5 year advancements in gold-enabled photocyclizations to build up diverse carbo- and heterocyclic scaffolds. This review offers comprehensive insights into mechanistic aspects and their significance in contemporary synthetic chemistry.

item.dc:title: Gold‐Catalyzed Photoannulation: Access to Cyclic Scaffolds

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Influence of 4,6‐O‐Benzylidine Towards α‐Galactosylation, Overcoming Neighbouring Group Participation Effect: An Unanticipated Observation With Viable Solution
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
During N‐iodosuccinimide and trimethylsilyl trifluoromethanesulfonate‐mediated β‐galactosylation, an unexpected formation of α‐glycoside was observed with a series of D‐Glucosamine‐derived acceptors. The unexpected stereo outcome was attributed to the 4,6‐O‐benzylidine in the donor molecule, and the expected β‐glycoside was formed upon removal of this benzylidine protecting group.
The construction of β‐glycosidic linkages with the assistance of neighbouring group participation (NGP) during N‐iodosuccinimide and trimethylsilyl trifluoromethanesulfonate‐mediated glycosylation reactions is considered one of the most reliable reactions. In this study, we demonstrated that the 4,6‐O‐benzylidine protection in the galactosyl thioglycoside donor can overshadow the NGP effect, furnishing the unexpected α‐glycoside with a specific set of acceptors and donors. We synthesized an orthogonally protected galactosyl thioglycoside donor to overcome the unexpected stereoselectivity. The outcome revealed that the presence of 4,6‐O‐benzylidine protection on the donor moiety can alter the stereoselectivity during glycosylation in certain cases.
item.content:encoded:
During N-iodosuccinimide and trimethylsilyl trifluoromethanesulfonate-mediated β-galactosylation, an unexpected formation of α-glycoside was observed with a series of D-Glucosamine-derived acceptors. The unexpected stereo outcome was attributed to the 4,6-O-benzylidine in the donor molecule, and the expected β-glycoside was formed upon removal of this benzylidine protecting group.


The construction of β-glycosidic linkages with the assistance of neighbouring group participation (NGP) during N-iodosuccinimide and trimethylsilyl trifluoromethanesulfonate-mediated glycosylation reactions is considered one of the most reliable reactions. In this study, we demonstrated that the 4,6-O-benzylidine protection in the galactosyl thioglycoside donor can overshadow the NGP effect, furnishing the unexpected α-glycoside with a specific set of acceptors and donors. We synthesized an orthogonally protected galactosyl thioglycoside donor to overcome the unexpected stereoselectivity. The outcome revealed that the presence of 4,6-O-benzylidine protection on the donor moiety can alter the stereoselectivity during glycosylation in certain cases.
item.dc:creator: Swapnendu Pramanik, Mrinmoy Manash Bharali, Abhishek Santra
item.dc:title: Influence of 4,6‐O‐Benzylidine Towards α‐Galactosylation, Overcoming Neighbouring Group Participation Effect: An Unanticipated Observation With Viable Solution

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Photocatalytic Synthesis of Imidazoline and Oxazolidine Derivatives Using Organic Catalysts
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Herein, we report a synthetic strategy that relies on a self‐developed organic photocatalyst for the efficient construction of imidazolines and oxazolidines. Using readily available 4‐methyl‐N‐(2 phenylallyl)benzenesulfonamide alkenes and redox‐active esters (RAEs) or aryl diazonium tetrafluoroborates as substrates, the reaction proceeds in acetonitrile/acetone via photoredox‐initiated radical addition followed by ionic cyclization, which involves the formation of a key nitrilium/imidate‐type ionic intermediate. This transformation features mild reaction conditions and facile operation, not only expanding the application scope of the developed photocatalyst, but also providing a practical technical platform for the photocatalytic synthesis of structurally diverse bioactive molecules containing imidazoline and oxazolidine motifs.

Using readily available 4‐methyl‐N‐(2‐phenylallyl)benzenesulfonamide alkenes and redox‐active esters (RAEs) or aryl diazonium tetrafluoroborates as substrates, the reaction proceeds in acetonitrile/acetone via photoredox‐initiated radical addition followed by ionic cyclization, which involves the formation of a key nitrilium/imidate‐type ionic intermediate. This transformation features mild reaction conditions and facile operation, not only expanding the application scope of the developed photocatalyst, but also providing a practical technical platform for the photocatalytic synthesis of structurally diverse bioactive molecules containing imidazoline and oxazolidine motifs.

Using readily available 4-methyl-N-(2 phenylallyl)benzenesulfonamide alkenes and redox-active esters (RAEs) or aryl diazonium tetrafluoroborates as substrates, the reaction proceeds in acetonitrile/acetone via photoredox-initiated radical addition followed by ionic cyclization, which involves the formation of a key nitrilium/imidate-type ionic intermediate. This transformation features mild reaction conditions and facile operation, not only expanding the application scope of the developed photocatalyst, but also providing a practical technical platform for the photocatalytic synthesis of structurally diverse bioactive molecules containing imidazoline and oxazolidine motifs.


Herein, we report a synthetic strategy that relies on a self-developed organic photocatalyst for the efficient construction of imidazolines and oxazolidines. Using readily available 4-methyl-N-(2-phenylallyl)benzenesulfonamide alkenes and redox-active esters (RAEs) or aryl diazonium tetrafluoroborates as substrates, the reaction proceeds in acetonitrile/acetone via photoredox-initiated radical addition followed by ionic cyclization, which involves the formation of a key nitrilium/imidate-type ionic intermediate. This transformation features mild reaction conditions and facile operation, not only expanding the application scope of the developed photocatalyst, but also providing a practical technical platform for the photocatalytic synthesis of structurally diverse bioactive molecules containing imidazoline and oxazolidine motifs.

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Difluorocarbene as C Synthon and CF Synthon in Cycloadditions and Annulations
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
This review summarizes the progress since 2018 in the application of difluorocarbene as either a C1 synthon or a CF synthon in cyclization reactions. The content is organized according to the distinct functions of difluorocarbene, provides a detailed overview of its role as C1 synthon for the synthesis of over twenty distinct heterocyclic scaffolds, and as CF synthon for the construction of monofluorinated heterocyclic frameworks, such as 2‐fluoroindoles, 3‐fluorinated oxindoles and indolizines.
This review summarizes the progress in the application of difluorocarbene as a multifunctional fluorinated reagent in cyclization reactions via the cleavage of C–F bonds, with a focus on research since 2018. The contents are organized according to the functions exhibited by difluorocarbene, and reaction mechanisms are provided to illustrate the multifaceted roles of this important building block in various transformations by selective C–F bond cleavage, including its use as a C1 synthon or CF synthon.
item.content:encoded:

This review summarizes the progress in the application of difluorocarbene as a multifunctional fluorinated reagent in cyclization reactions via the cleavage of C–F bonds, with a focus on research since 2018. The contents are organized according to the functions exhibited by difluorocarbene, and reaction mechanisms are provided to illustrate the multifaceted roles of this important building block in various transformations by selective C–F bond cleavage, including its use as a C1 synthon or CF synthon.
